title: New Apixaban Prescription Counselling
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. Mrs. Linda Chen, age 68, has arrived to pick up a new prescription for apixaban 5 mg twice daily, newly diagnosed with atrial fibrillation by her cardiologist last week. She has never taken a blood thinner before. Her task for you today is to counsel her on this new medication before she leaves the pharmacy. Address her questions and concerns as they arise during the conversation.
materials_provided: Prescription: Apixaban 5 mg PO BID, quantity 60, 3 refills. Patient profile: age 68, weight 71 kg, creatinine 88 umol/L, history of atrial fibrillation, hypertension (on ramipril 10 mg daily), no known drug allergies, no other anticoagulants on file.
sp_role_script: You are Linda Chen, 68 years old, recently told by your cardiologist that you have an irregular heartbeat and need to start a blood thinner to prevent stroke. You are anxious because your late husband had a bad bleeding event on warfarin years ago and you associate all blood thinners with that memory. When the pharmacist enters, say you are nervous about starting this new pill. Ask these questions in the conversation: 1) Do I need to get my blood tested regularly like my husband did on warfarin. 2) What happens if I cut myself, will I bleed to death. 3) Can I still take my ibuprofen for my knee pain. 4) What do I do if I miss a dose. 5) Are there any foods I need to avoid. If the candidate reassures you calmly and explains clearly that routine INR monitoring is not required for apixaban, visibly relax and thank them. If the candidate is dismissive of your fear or does not acknowledge it, remain visibly tense and ask the same question again in different words.

checklist.item: Introduces self and confirms patient identity and the medication before beginning counselling
Acknowledges the patient's anxiety about bleeding before moving to factual information
Uses plain, non-technical language and checks understanding at least once
Correctly explains that routine INR or coagulation monitoring is not required for apixaban unlike warfarin
Explains the bleeding risk accurately without minimizing it, and gives clear instructions on what to do for minor versus serious bleeding
Advises against regular ibuprofen or other NSAID use due to additive bleeding risk and suggests acetaminophen as an alternative for knee pain
Gives correct missed dose instructions specific to a twice-daily anticoagulant
Demonstrates a organized, patient-centred counselling structure and closes by inviting further questions

model_answer: A strong candidate opens by introducing themselves, confirming the patient's identity and the new prescription, then explicitly acknowledges Mrs. Chen's fear rooted in her husband's warfarin experience before explaining anything. They clarify that apixaban does not require routine blood monitoring the way warfarin does, which directly addresses her first question. They explain bleeding risk honestly, describing what minor bleeding looks like versus red-flag signs like black stools or severe headache that need urgent care. They advise against regular NSAID use with apixaban and recommend acetaminophen instead, and give the correct missed-dose instruction for a twice-daily agent. Throughout, they check her understanding and close by confirming she feels comfortable starting the medication.
references_provided: Excerpt from the current product monograph summary table showing apixaban dosing, missed dose instructions, and drug interaction cautions.

title: New Insulin Regimen Counselling
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. Mr. Dave Okafor, age 54, is picking up his first insulin prescription, insulin glargine 10 units at bedtime, added to his existing metformin for poorly controlled type 2 diabetes. He has never injected insulin before. Your task is to counsel him on how to store, prepare, inject, and dispose of his insulin, and to check his readiness to self-administer before he leaves.
materials_provided: Prescription: Insulin glargine (Lantus) 100 units/mL, FlexTouch pen, 10 units subcutaneously at bedtime. Patient profile: type 2 diabetes for 8 years, A1c 9.2 percent, currently on metformin 1000 mg BID, no known allergies, has pen needles and a sharps container dispensed today.
sp_role_script: You are Dave Okafor, 54, a truck driver who is uneasy about needles and has been putting off starting insulin for months because his coworker told him insulin means his diabetes is now serious and out of control. When the pharmacist enters, mention you are not thrilled about this and ask if it means you failed at managing your diabetes. Raise these points during the conversation: 1) Where on my body am I supposed to inject this. 2) Do I need to refrigerate the pen once I start using it. 3) What if I take too much or forget a dose, especially since I drive long hours. 4) How do I get rid of the used needles safely. 5) Will this make my blood sugar drop while I am driving. Show mild relief once the pharmacist normalizes insulin as a normal part of diabetes progression rather than a personal failure, and pay close attention to the injection technique explanation since you are anxious about doing it wrong.

checklist.item: Introduces self, confirms identity and the new prescription, and normalizes starting insulin without judgment
Directly addresses the patient's feeling that needing insulin means he failed, reframing diabetes as progressive
Correctly describes appropriate injection sites and the need to rotate sites to prevent lipohypertrophy
Correctly explains storage: unopened pens refrigerated, in-use pen kept at room temperature and discarded after the labeled in-use period
Explains recognition and management of hypoglycemia symptoms relevant to a commercial driver, including timing insulin relative to bedtime
Provides correct missed dose guidance for basal insulin
Gives correct safe sharps disposal instructions using the provided sharps container
Confirms patient comfort and readiness to self-inject before ending the encounter, offering a return visit or demonstration if needed

model_answer: A strong candidate begins by normalizing the need for insulin as a natural part of type 2 diabetes progression rather than personal failure, which directly addresses Mr. Okafor's emotional concern. They explain correct injection technique and site rotation across the abdomen, thigh, and upper arm, and clarify that unopened pens are refrigerated while the in-use pen stays at room temperature and is discarded after its labeled duration. They cover hypoglycemia symptoms and stress taking the bedtime dose consistently rather than while driving, along with correct missed-dose guidance for a once-daily basal insulin. They finish with clear sharps disposal instructions and confirm he feels ready to give his first injection, offering to demonstrate again if needed.
references_provided: Manufacturer patient insulin pen instruction sheet showing injection site diagram and in-use storage duration.

title: New Inhaler Technique for COPD
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. Mr. Tom Bishop, age 71, has been prescribed a new tiotropium dry powder inhaler (Spiriva HandiHaler) for newly diagnosed moderate COPD, in addition to his existing salbutamol rescue inhaler. He has never used a dry powder inhaler before. Your task is to counsel him on the purpose of this maintenance inhaler versus his rescue inhaler and to teach and confirm correct HandiHaler technique.
materials_provided: Prescription: Tiotropium bromide 18 mcg HandiHaler, one capsule inhaled once daily. Patient profile: newly diagnosed moderate COPD, long-time smoker, existing salbutamol MDI PRN, no known allergies, demonstration HandiHaler device and placebo capsule available at station.
sp_role_script: You are Tom Bishop, 71, a retired mechanic who has smoked for 40 years and was recently told he has COPD. You are confused about why you need two different inhalers and worry this new one is stronger medicine for worse lungs. When the pharmacist enters, mention you already have your blue puffer and ask why you need another one. During the conversation raise: 1) Is this new one for when I am short of breath right now. 2) How is this capsule thing supposed to work, do I swallow it. 3) How often do I use it. 4) Can I use my old inhaler technique for this one too. 5) What if I forget a dose. Show genuine confusion about device technique so the candidate has an opportunity to demonstrate and have you attempt the technique back. React positively when the difference between maintenance and rescue therapy is explained clearly with a simple analogy.

checklist.item: Introduces self, confirms identity, and asks what the patient currently understands about his new inhaler before explaining
Uses a clear analogy to differentiate maintenance therapy from rescue therapy
Correctly explains tiotropium is a once-daily maintenance bronchodilator and is not for acute shortness of breath, and salbutamol remains the rescue inhaler
Correctly demonstrates or describes HandiHaler technique including piercing the capsule once, inhaling slowly and deeply, and not swallowing the capsule
Has the patient demonstrate technique back or confirms verbal teach-back of the steps
Gives correct missed dose guidance for a once-daily maintenance inhaler
Confirms the patient can distinguish which inhaler to use in which situation before ending the encounter

model_answer: A strong candidate first asks what Mr. Bishop already understands, then clearly distinguishes tiotropium as a once-daily maintenance medication that keeps airways open over time, while salbutamol remains his rescue inhaler for sudden shortness of breath. They demonstrate correct HandiHaler technique, placing the capsule in the chamber, piercing it once, and inhaling slowly and deeply rather than swallowing it, and ask him to repeat the technique to confirm understanding. They give accurate missed-dose guidance for the once-daily regimen and reassure him this is standard COPD management rather than a sign of worsening disease. The encounter ends with confirmation he knows which device to reach for in each situation.
references_provided: HandiHaler device and placebo capsule for technique demonstration; manufacturer patient instruction card.

title: New Hydromorphone Prescription After Surgery
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. Ms. Priya Anand, age 42, is picking up a new prescription for hydromorphone 2 mg tablets, one to two tablets every 4 hours as needed for pain, quantity 20, following a discharge from hospital after abdominal surgery three days ago. Your task is to counsel her on safe and appropriate use of this opioid, including risks, storage, and disposal, while assessing for appropriateness given her profile.
materials_provided: Prescription: Hydromorphone 2 mg PO q4h PRN pain, quantity 20, no refills. Patient profile: post-operative day 3 after laparoscopic cholecystectomy, also filled docusate and senna today, drinks alcohol socially on weekends, drives to work daily, no known allergies, lives with her teenage son.
sp_role_script: You are Priya Anand, 42, three days post-surgery and still in noticeable pain but eager to get back to normal life quickly. You are a bit embarrassed about needing a strong opioid and downplay how much pain you are actually in. When the pharmacist enters, say you just want to grab your prescription and go. During the conversation raise: 1) Can I have a glass of wine with dinner tonight since I am off work. 2) I have a big presentation in two days, can I drive after taking this. 3) My son sometimes goes through my things, should I be worried. 4) I heard opioids are addictive, will I get hooked after just using it for a few days. 5) Do I really need to take the stool softener too, my bowels are fine. Express some impatience if the candidate spends too long without addressing your actual questions, but soften and engage more when they explain risks in a non-judgmental, practical way.

checklist.item: Introduces self, confirms identity and the prescription, and creates a private, non-judgmental tone appropriate for opioid counselling
Directly and respectfully addresses the patient's minimization of her pain and her embarrassment without lecturing
Correctly advises against combining hydromorphone with alcohol due to additive CNS and respiratory depression risk
Correctly advises not to drive or operate machinery until she knows how the medication affects her, given sedation risk
Provides accurate, reassuring information on short-term opioid use and addiction risk, distinguishing physical dependence from addiction
Recommends secure storage away from her teenage son and correct disposal of unused tablets
Correctly explains the purpose of concurrent docusate and senna for opioid-induced constipation prevention
Balances safety counselling with respect for the patient's autonomy and time, closing with an offer for further questions

model_answer: A strong candidate creates a private, respectful tone and gently acknowledges that Ms. Anand may be minimizing her pain, without making her feel judged. They clearly advise against alcohol due to additive sedation and respiratory depression risk, and advise against driving until she knows how the medication affects her, directly relevant to her upcoming presentation. They reassure her that short courses of opioids for acute post-surgical pain carry low addiction risk while distinguishing this from physical dependence, and recommend she store the tablets securely and out of reach of her son, with instructions for returning unused tablets to the pharmacy for disposal. They explain that docusate and senna prevent opioid-induced constipation even before it starts, and close by inviting any remaining questions.
references_provided: Hospital discharge summary noting laparoscopic cholecystectomy on postoperative day 3.

title: New Digoxin Prescription Counselling
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. Mr. George Petrov, age 79, is picking up a new prescription for digoxin 0.125 mg once daily for heart failure with atrial fibrillation, added to his existing furosemide and ramipril. Your task is to counsel him on this narrow-therapeutic-index medication, including signs of toxicity, monitoring needs, and interactions relevant to his other medications, and to check his understanding before he leaves.
materials_provided: Prescription: Digoxin 0.125 mg PO once daily, quantity 30, 2 refills. Patient profile: age 79, weight 68 kg, creatinine 132 umol/L, current medications furosemide 40 mg daily and ramipril 5 mg daily, most recent potassium 3.4 mmol/L, no known allergies.
sp_role_script: You are George Petrov, 79, a retired accountant who likes precise information and reads medication inserts carefully, which sometimes makes you more anxious rather than less. You have just read online that digoxin can be dangerous in older adults and are worried the pharmacist is not telling you everything. When the pharmacist enters, say you read some concerning things about this medication online and want the full picture. During the conversation raise: 1) What are the actual signs that this medication is building up too much in my body. 2) Do I need blood tests, and if so how often. 3) I take a water pill too, does that matter. 4) I sometimes take an antacid for heartburn, is that a problem. 5) What happens if I miss a dose, should I double up next time. Respond with visible relief and nodding when the pharmacist gives specific, concrete answers rather than vague reassurance, and press further with follow-up if an answer feels evasive.

checklist.item: Introduces self, confirms identity, and responds to the patient's anxiety with specific rather than vague reassurance
Checks understanding with teach-back given the patient's detail-oriented, anxious communication style
Correctly lists key digoxin toxicity signs such as nausea, vomiting, visual disturbances, confusion, and arrhythmia symptoms
Correctly explains the need for periodic renal function and electrolyte monitoring, particularly potassium, and digoxin level checks when indicated
Correctly identifies that furosemide-induced hypokalemia increases the risk of digoxin toxicity and explains why this matters
Correctly counsels on antacid interaction, advising spacing administration to avoid reduced digoxin absorption
Gives correct missed dose instruction and explicitly advises against doubling the dose
Demonstrates organized, precise counselling appropriate to a narrow-therapeutic-index drug and closes by confirming the patient feels informed

model_answer: A strong candidate responds to Mr. Petrov's anxiety with specific, concrete information rather than general reassurance, which builds his trust. They clearly list digoxin toxicity signs including nausea, visual changes such as yellow-green halos, confusion, and irregular heartbeat, and explain the need for periodic kidney function, potassium, and digoxin level monitoring. They connect his furosemide use to an increased toxicity risk through low potassium, and advise spacing his antacid apart from his digoxin dose to preserve absorption. They give the correct missed-dose instruction, explicitly warning against doubling up, and close with teach-back to confirm he can identify warning signs that should prompt him to call the pharmacy or his doctor.
references_provided: Excerpt from renal and electrolyte monitoring lab values on the patient profile.

title: Pediatric Amoxicillin Suspension Dosing
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. Mrs. Amara Diallo has arrived to pick up a new prescription of amoxicillin suspension for her 4-year-old son Kofi, who weighs 18 kg, for acute otitis media. Your task is to counsel her on correct measuring, dosing, administration, and duration, and to confirm the prescribed dose is appropriate for the child's weight before dispensing.
materials_provided: Prescription: Amoxicillin 400 mg/5 mL suspension, give 10 mL PO twice daily for 10 days. Patient profile: Kofi Diallo, age 4, weight 18 kg, diagnosed with acute otitis media today, no known drug allergies, no other current medications.
sp_role_script: You are Amara Diallo, mother of 4-year-old Kofi who has an ear infection and has been fussy and not sleeping well, which has left you tired and slightly distracted during this conversation. When the pharmacist enters, mention Kofi hates taking medicine and always spits it out. During the conversation raise: 1) How am I supposed to actually get him to take this without a huge fight. 2) Do I use a kitchen spoon since that's what I have at home. 3) Does it matter if I give it with food. 4) What if he throws it up right after I give it. 5) How long until he starts feeling better and can go back to daycare. If the candidate calculates or confirms the dose against Kofi's weight and explains it clearly, express relief that the amount seems right. If the candidate does not check the weight-based dose at all, ask directly whether this is definitely the right amount for a boy his size.

checklist.item: Introduces self, confirms identity of the caregiver and the child, and acknowledges the caregiver's fatigue and the child's medication aversion with empathy
Verifies the prescribed dose against the child's weight using appropriate mg per kg per day dosing for otitis media and confirms it is appropriate
Correctly instructs use of an oral dosing syringe or calibrated device rather than a household spoon
Provides practical administration tips appropriate for a reluctant preschooler, such as giving with a small amount of food or a preferred drink if not contraindicated
Gives correct guidance on what to do if the child vomits shortly after a dose
Counsels on completing the full 10-day course even if symptoms improve early
Advises on realistic expected timeline for symptom improvement and general return-to-daycare guidance
Communicates in a warm, practical, non-judgmental manner suited to a tired caregiver and confirms she feels able to manage administration

model_answer: A strong candidate begins by acknowledging how exhausting it can be to medicate a fussy preschooler, which builds rapport with Mrs. Diallo. They confirm the 10 mL twice-daily dose is appropriate for Kofi's 18 kg weight using standard pediatric mg per kg dosing for otitis media, directly reassuring her the amount is correct. They instruct her to use an oral dosing syringe rather than a kitchen spoon for accuracy, and offer practical tips such as giving the dose with a small amount of food or a favourite drink to improve acceptance. They explain what to do if he vomits shortly after dosing, stress the importance of completing the full 10 days even once he feels better, and give a realistic timeline for improvement, closing by confirming she feels confident administering the medication.
references_provided: Pediatric dosing reference chart showing amoxicillin dosing by weight for acute otitis media.

title: Geriatric Polypharmacy Review with New Prescription
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. Mrs. Eleanor Whitfield, age 84, is picking up a new prescription for a low-dose sleep aid, zopiclone 3.75 mg at bedtime, prescribed by a walk-in clinic doctor she saw last week for insomnia. She already takes six other regular medications. Your task is to counsel her on the new prescription while considering her full medication list and raising any concerns before she leaves with it.
materials_provided: Prescription: Zopiclone 3.75 mg PO at bedtime PRN for insomnia, quantity 15. Patient profile: age 84, lives alone, history of falls in the past year, current medications are amlodipine 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily, metformin 500 mg BID, oxybutynin 5 mg BID, ramipril 2.5 mg daily, and acetaminophen 500 mg PRN, no known allergies.
sp_role_script: You are Eleanor Whitfield, 84, living alone, who has not been sleeping well for weeks and is desperate for anything that will help. You are somewhat hard of hearing and ask the pharmacist to repeat things occasionally. You do not think of your sleep medication as risky since your doctor gave it to you. When the pharmacist enters, mention you are looking forward to finally getting a good night's sleep. During the conversation raise: 1) Why would this be a problem, my doctor already said it was fine. 2) I already take a pill for my bladder, does that matter. 3) Can I get up in the night to use the bathroom like I usually do after taking this. 4) How long can I keep taking this if it works well. 5) Can I have my usual evening glass of sherry with it. Show some defensiveness if the candidate implies she should not have been prescribed this at all, but become more receptive if they explain fall risk and interactions clearly and offer practical safety strategies instead of just saying no.

checklist.item: Introduces self, confirms identity, speaks clearly and checks understanding given her hearing difficulty
Validates her desire for better sleep before raising safety concerns, avoiding a dismissive or lecturing tone
Correctly identifies increased fall risk from zopiclone combined with her history of falls and age
Correctly identifies the additive anticholinergic and sedative burden with oxybutynin and explains the relevance
Advises against combining zopiclone with alcohol due to additive CNS depression
Recommends practical nighttime safety strategies for a patient at fall risk, such as clearing pathways and using a bedside light before getting up
Advises that zopiclone is intended for short-term use only and recommends follow-up with her prescriber regarding duration and non-pharmacologic options
Balances patient autonomy and respect for the prescriber's decision with clear, actionable safety counselling, and offers to communicate with her prescriber if appropriate

model_answer: A strong candidate validates Mrs. Whitfield's frustration with poor sleep before raising concerns, avoiding language that undermines her prescriber. They explain that zopiclone increases her fall risk given her age and prior falls, and that combined with her oxybutynin it adds to sedation and anticholinergic burden, both relevant to her living alone. They advise against her usual evening sherry while on this medication due to additive sedation, and give practical nighttime safety tips such as keeping a clear path and a light within reach if she needs to get up. They explain zopiclone is meant for short-term use and suggest she follow up with her prescriber about duration and other options for chronic insomnia, offering to fax or call the clinic with her concerns if she consents.
references_provided: Complete medication profile listing all six current medications with doses.

title: New Prescription in Pregnancy for Nausea
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. Ms. Rachel Simmons, age 29, who is 9 weeks pregnant, is picking up a new prescription for doxylamine-pyridoxine (Diclectin) for nausea and vomiting of pregnancy. Your task is to counsel her on how to take this medication correctly, its safety in pregnancy, and address any concerns she raises about taking medication while pregnant.
materials_provided: Prescription: Doxylamine 10 mg / pyridoxine 10 mg delayed-release tablets, start with 2 tablets at bedtime, may increase per labeled titration schedule up to 4 tablets daily. Patient profile: 9 weeks pregnant, first pregnancy, no known allergies, taking a prenatal vitamin, no other regular medications.
sp_role_script: You are Rachel Simmons, 29, nine weeks into your first pregnancy and feeling nauseated most of the day, which is affecting your ability to work. You are very cautious about taking any medication and have been reading conflicting things online about pregnancy safety. When the pharmacist enters, say you have been miserable with nausea but are scared to take anything that could hurt the baby. During the conversation raise: 1) Is this actually safe for the baby, I have read scary things about morning sickness pills in the past. 2) Do I take this only when I feel sick or every day. 3) Will this make me too drowsy to drive to work. 4) Can I take my regular ginger tea and vitamin B6 gummies along with this. 5) How long will I need to take this. Show visible relief when the pharmacist explains the strong safety evidence and the titration schedule clearly, and ask a clarifying follow-up if the timing instructions are not concrete.

checklist.item: Introduces self, confirms identity, and responds to her fear about medication safety in pregnancy with empathy before giving facts
Correctly reassures with accurate safety information, noting this combination is a first-line, well-studied option for nausea and vomiting of pregnancy
Correctly explains the scheduled dosing and titration approach rather than as-needed use only
Correctly counsels on sedation risk and advises caution with driving or activities requiring alertness, especially when starting or increasing the dose
Correctly addresses concurrent use of her separate vitamin B6 supplement to avoid inadvertent double dosing of pyridoxine
Provides appropriate guidance on duration of use and follow-up with her prenatal care provider if symptoms persist or worsen
Communicates with warmth and reassurance appropriate to an anxious pregnant patient and confirms she feels comfortable starting the medication

model_answer: A strong candidate acknowledges Ms. Simmons's fear about medication safety in pregnancy before presenting facts, which helps her feel heard. They reassure her that doxylamine-pyridoxine is a well-studied, first-line treatment recommended for nausea and vomiting of pregnancy, and explain the scheduled bedtime-start titration rather than as-needed dosing. They counsel her on possible drowsiness, advising caution with driving especially when starting or adjusting the dose, and clarify that she should discuss her separate vitamin B6 gummies with the pharmacist to avoid excess pyridoxine intake. They note the medication can be continued as needed throughout the pregnancy under her provider's guidance and encourage follow-up if symptoms worsen, closing with reassurance that addresses her original fear directly.
references_provided: Excerpt from a Canadian pregnancy-safe medication reference summarizing evidence for doxylamine-pyridoxine in pregnancy.

title: New Statin Prescription with Patient Wanting to Stop Early
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. Mr. Samuel Kwan, age 58, is picking up a refill of atorvastatin 40 mg daily, started three months ago for high cholesterol after a cardiac risk assessment. He mentions he is thinking about stopping it. Your task is to explore his concerns, address any misconceptions, and counsel him on the importance of continued therapy while respecting his right to make an informed decision.
materials_provided: Prescription: Atorvastatin 40 mg PO once daily, refill quantity 90. Patient profile: age 58, started statin 3 months ago following elevated LDL and moderate 10-year cardiovascular risk score, no known allergies, drinks alcohol occasionally, no other regular medications.
sp_role_script: You are Samuel Kwan, 58, who started a statin three months ago but has been having some muscle soreness after workouts and read online that statins cause serious muscle damage and liver failure. You have decided you would rather manage your cholesterol with diet alone and are picking up this refill mostly out of habit while planning to quietly stop taking it. When the pharmacist enters, mention you are not sure you want to keep taking this. During the conversation raise: 1) I read statins can destroy your liver and muscles, is that true. 2) Can't I just fix this with diet and exercise instead. 3) The soreness started right after I began the pill, isn't that proof it's causing it. 4) What tests would even show if it's a real problem. 5) If I stop now would my risk really go up that much. Become more open to continuing if the candidate takes the muscle symptom seriously, explains realistic risk versus benefit clearly, and offers to look into it rather than dismissing it outright.

checklist.item: Introduces self, confirms identity, and explores the patient's reasons for wanting to stop with genuine curiosity rather than judgment
Validates the muscle soreness as a legitimate concern worth investigating rather than dismissing it
Correctly addresses the misconception that statins commonly cause serious liver failure, giving accurate information on actual incidence and monitoring
Appropriately distinguishes benign muscle soreness from statin-associated myopathy and outlines when creatine kinase testing or medical follow-up is warranted
Explains realistic cardiovascular risk reduction benefit of continued statin therapy given his risk profile, without exaggerating or minimizing
Discusses the role of diet and exercise as complementary rather than a full replacement for statin therapy given his risk level
Offers a concrete next step such as following up with his prescriber about the muscle symptoms rather than unilaterally stopping the medication
Respects the patient's autonomy to make the final decision while ensuring it is an informed one, and closes with a clear plan

model_answer: A strong candidate explores Mr. Kwan's reasoning without judgment and takes his muscle soreness seriously rather than brushing it off. They correct the misconception that statins commonly cause serious liver failure, explaining that significant liver injury is rare and routinely monitored, and they distinguish common mild myalgia from rare statin-associated myopathy, suggesting a creatine kinase test or prescriber follow-up if symptoms persist. They explain his personal cardiovascular risk reduction benefit honestly and frame diet and exercise as complementary to, rather than a replacement for, his statin given his risk score. They propose a concrete plan, such as contacting his prescriber about the muscle symptoms before stopping outright, respecting his autonomy while ensuring his decision is fully informed.
references_provided: Cardiovascular risk assessment summary from the patient's chart showing his 10-year risk score.

title: New Methotrexate Weekly Dosing for Rheumatoid Arthritis
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. Ms. Fatima Rahman, age 46, is picking up a new prescription for oral methotrexate 15 mg once weekly for newly diagnosed rheumatoid arthritis, along with folic acid 5 mg once weekly on a different day. Your task is to counsel her thoroughly on the once-weekly dosing schedule for this narrow-therapeutic-index medication, since dosing errors with methotrexate can be fatal, and confirm she understands before dispensing.
materials_provided: Prescription: Methotrexate 2.5 mg tablets, take 6 tablets (15 mg) once weekly on the same day each week, plus folic acid 5 mg once weekly on a different day. Patient profile: newly diagnosed rheumatoid arthritis, no known allergies, drinks alcohol rarely, no other regular medications, baseline liver enzymes and complete blood count on file as normal.
sp_role_script: You are Fatima Rahman, 46, newly diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis and overwhelmed by the diagnosis and the new medication regimen. You are used to taking medications daily and assume this one works the same way unless told otherwise. When the pharmacist enters, mention you already started organizing a daily pillbox for this new medication. During the conversation raise: 1) So I just take this every day like my other pills, right. 2) What is the folic acid for, do I take it the same day as the other pill. 3) What happens if I accidentally take it daily by mistake. 4) Can I drink wine on the weekends still. 5) What symptoms would mean something serious is going wrong. React with alarm and gratitude when the candidate corrects the daily-dosing assumption, since you realize you almost made a dangerous error, and ask them to help you set up a clear weekly schedule.

checklist.item: Introduces self, confirms identity, and proactively checks the patient's understanding of dosing frequency rather than assuming she knows it is weekly
Responds calmly and supportively when she reveals she almost took it daily, without inducing panic
Clearly and unambiguously establishes that this methotrexate dose is taken once weekly, not daily, and helps her identify a specific day to take it consistently
Correctly explains that folic acid is taken once weekly on a different day than the methotrexate to reduce methotrexate side effects
Correctly explains the serious danger of inadvertent daily dosing and what to do if a dosing error occurs
Correctly counsels on alcohol avoidance or significant limitation due to additive hepatotoxicity risk
Correctly lists key warning signs requiring urgent medical attention such as unusual bruising, bleeding, mouth sores, shortness of breath, or signs of infection
Uses teach-back to confirm the patient can correctly state her weekly dosing schedule before ending the encounter

model_answer: A strong candidate does not assume Ms. Rahman understands the dosing frequency and proactively confirms it, uncovering that she was planning to take methotrexate daily, a potentially fatal error. They calmly and clearly establish that this dose is taken once weekly on a fixed day, and that folic acid is taken once weekly on a separate day to help reduce methotrexate toxicity. They explain why accidental daily dosing is dangerous and what to do if it happens, advise significantly limiting or avoiding alcohol due to added liver toxicity risk, and list key warning signs such as unusual bruising, mouth sores, or signs of infection that require urgent medical attention. They close with teach-back, having her state back her weekly schedule to confirm the dangerous misunderstanding has been fully corrected.
references_provided: Institute for Safe Medication Practices style warning excerpt on weekly-only oral methotrexate dosing errors.

title: New Asthma Inhaler with Steroid Side Effect Fear
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. Mr. Jason Whyte, age 24, is picking up a new prescription for budesonide-formoterol (Symbicort) 200/6 mcg, one inhalation twice daily, for moderate persistent asthma, in addition to his existing salbutamol rescue inhaler. Your task is to counsel him on correct use of this combination inhaler and address his stated hesitation about taking a steroid medication regularly.
materials_provided: Prescription: Budesonide-formoterol 200/6 mcg dry powder inhaler (Turbuhaler), one inhalation twice daily. Patient profile: age 24, diagnosed with moderate persistent asthma, currently uses salbutamol MDI several times per week, no known allergies, plays recreational hockey.
sp_role_script: You are Jason Whyte, 24, a recreational hockey player recently told your asthma is not well controlled and prescribed a new daily inhaler that contains a steroid. You are wary of steroids because a friend at the gym told you steroids cause weight gain, weakened bones, and stunted muscle growth, and you are worried about your athletic performance. When the pharmacist enters, say you are hesitant to start something with steroids in it every day. During the conversation raise: 1) Is this the same kind of steroid that bodybuilders get in trouble for using. 2) Will this affect my muscle growth or performance on the ice. 3) Do I still need my blue inhaler if I am using this new one. 4) What if I just use this one when I feel wheezy instead of every day. 5) Are there side effects I should watch for in my mouth or throat. Show noticeably reduced tension once the candidate clearly distinguishes an inhaled corticosteroid from anabolic steroids and explains the low systemic absorption, and ask a practical follow-up about rinsing after use.

checklist.item: Introduces self, confirms identity, and takes the patient's steroid concern seriously without dismissing it as uninformed
Clearly distinguishes inhaled corticosteroids from anabolic steroids and explains they work differently in the body
Correctly explains that at prescribed inhaled doses, systemic absorption and effects on muscle, bone, or performance are minimal
Correctly clarifies that salbutamol remains available as a rescue inhaler and this new combination inhaler is a scheduled maintenance therapy, not as-needed only
Correctly counsels on rinsing the mouth after each use to reduce risk of oral thrush and hoarseness
Demonstrates or describes correct Turbuhaler loading and inhalation technique
Confirms the patient's misconception is resolved and he feels comfortable starting daily use before ending the encounter

model_answer: A strong candidate takes Mr. Whyte's steroid concern seriously rather than brushing it aside, then clearly distinguishes inhaled corticosteroids from anabolic steroids, explaining they act locally in the lungs with minimal systemic absorption at prescribed doses and therefore have negligible effect on muscle growth or athletic performance. They clarify that this new inhaler is a scheduled twice-daily maintenance therapy and that his salbutamol remains his separate rescue inhaler for sudden symptoms, correcting his idea of using it only when wheezy. They counsel him to rinse his mouth after each use to prevent oral thrush and hoarseness and confirm correct Turbuhaler loading and inhalation technique. The encounter closes with confirmation that his original fear about steroids has been resolved and he is ready to start the regimen as prescribed.
references_provided: Turbuhaler device and manufacturer patient instruction card for technique demonstration.

title: New Sertraline Prescription with Early Discontinuation Concern
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. Ms. Olivia Bennett, age 33, is picking up a new prescription for sertraline 50 mg once daily for newly diagnosed generalized anxiety disorder and mild depression. Your task is to counsel her on how the medication works, what to expect in early treatment, and address her stated plan to stop taking it as soon as she feels better.
materials_provided: Prescription: Sertraline 50 mg PO once daily, quantity 30, 2 refills. Patient profile: age 33, newly diagnosed generalized anxiety disorder and mild depressive symptoms, no known allergies, no other regular medications, occasional evening glass of wine.
sp_role_script: You are Olivia Bennett, 33, recently diagnosed with anxiety and mild depression and prescribed your first antidepressant. You are determined to only take it until you feel back to normal, then stop, since you do not want to be dependent on medication long term. When the pharmacist enters, mention you plan to take this for a few weeks until you feel better and then stop. During the conversation raise: 1) Isn't it better to get off this as soon as possible once I feel okay. 2) How long until I actually start feeling different. 3) I heard antidepressants can make you feel worse before better, is that true. 4) Can I have a glass of wine at dinner while taking this. 5) What happens if I just stop taking it suddenly one day. Show a shift toward being more open to a longer treatment course once the candidate explains the delayed onset of benefit and the risks of stopping abruptly, but continue to seek reassurance that this is not a lifelong commitment.

checklist.item: Introduces self, confirms identity, and explores the patient's reasoning for wanting to stop early without judgment
Correctly explains the typical delayed onset of therapeutic benefit for sertraline, usually several weeks, and that early relief is not a signal to stop
Correctly describes common early side effects such as nausea or transient jitteriness and reassures these typically improve with continued use
Correctly explains the recommended minimum duration of treatment for a first episode before considering tapering, in consultation with her prescriber
Correctly explains the risk of discontinuation symptoms with abrupt cessation and the need for a gradual taper under medical guidance
Appropriately counsels on moderate alcohol use while taking an SSRI without being unnecessarily restrictive
Reassures the patient that treatment duration is a shared decision with her prescriber rather than an indefinite commitment, closing with a clear plan to follow up

model_answer: A strong candidate explores Ms. Bennett's motivation for wanting to stop early without judgment, then explains that sertraline typically takes several weeks to show full benefit, so early improvement is not a cue to discontinue. They describe common early side effects like mild nausea or jitteriness as usually transient, and explain that guidelines generally recommend continuing treatment for a first episode for a number of months after symptoms improve before considering any taper, always in consultation with her prescriber. They clearly explain that stopping abruptly can cause discontinuation symptoms and that any stopping should be done gradually under medical supervision, and give balanced, non-restrictive guidance on her occasional wine. They close by framing treatment duration as a collaborative decision with her prescriber rather than a lifelong commitment, directly addressing her underlying worry.
references_provided: Canadian clinical practice guideline excerpt summarizing recommended minimum treatment duration for a first episode of generalized anxiety disorder or depression.

title: Cough and Cold Complaint with Hidden Red Flag
setting: A community pharmacy counter during a busy weekday afternoon.
candidate_instructions: A patient approaches the counter asking for something for a cough that has lasted just over three weeks. He mentions it started after a cold but never fully went away. He is asking to buy a dextromethorphan and pseudoephedrine combination product he saw advertised. Assess his complaint, ask enough questions to identify any red flags, and recommend an appropriate course of action, including whether self-care is still suitable or referral is needed.
materials_provided: None beyond the instructions above.
sp_role_script: You are a 58 year old man who has had a cough for three and a half weeks. It started with a cold but the cold symptoms went away after a week and the cough never stopped. You have lost some weight without trying, maybe 4 kilograms in the last month, but you assume it is from being too busy to eat properly. You have smoked about a pack a day for 30 years. You have not seen a doctor because you hate waiting rooms. Your opening line is asking for the cough and cold product by name and saying you just need something strong to get rid of it. If asked about duration, mention it clearly. If asked about smoking, admit it but downplay it. If asked about weight loss, mention it casually as if unimportant. If pushed toward seeing a doctor, express reluctance and say you are too busy, showing mild irritation, but agree if the candidate explains clearly why it matters.

checklist.item: Greets the patient and establishes the reason for the visit in a clear, non-judgmental manner
Uses open-ended questions to gather a focused cough history
Identifies cough duration greater than three weeks as a red flag requiring referral
Identifies smoking history and unintentional weight loss as additional red flags
Does not simply sell the requested OTC product without addressing the red flags
Recommends prompt medical referral and explains the rationale in plain language
Demonstrates appropriate urgency without causing undue alarm
Overall clinical reasoning and patient-centred communication

model_answer: A strong candidate opens by acknowledging the patient's request, then takes a focused history covering duration, associated symptoms, smoking history, and any weight changes. On hearing that the cough has lasted over three weeks alongside unintentional weight loss and a significant smoking history, the candidate recognizes these as red flags inconsistent with a simple post-viral cough. The candidate clearly but gently explains why these findings warrant medical assessment rather than an OTC product alone, avoiding alarming language while conveying appropriate urgency. The candidate offers a short-term suggestion for comfort if appropriate but frames the referral as the priority, and confirms the patient understands the plan before closing.

title: Heartburn Complaint Requesting Ongoing Antacid Use
candidate_instructions: A patient asks for a refill recommendation of an antacid she has been using almost daily for the past two months to manage heartburn after meals. Explore her symptoms, assess for red flags suggesting a condition beyond simple heartburn, and provide an appropriate self-care or referral recommendation.
materials_provided: None beyond the instructions above.
sp_role_script: You are a 44 year old woman who has had burning chest discomfort after eating for about two months, occurring four to five times a week, usually worse lying down at night. You have been using an antacid liquid almost every day and it helps a little but not fully. You have not had any difficulty swallowing, no vomiting blood, and no black stools. You have noticed the symptoms are getting slightly more frequent. Your opening line is asking whether you can just keep buying the same antacid or if there is something stronger over the counter. If asked about frequency and duration, answer honestly showing it is a chronic pattern. If asked about alarm symptoms, deny them clearly. If the candidate recommends seeing a doctor, ask why, since the antacid has been helping somewhat, showing mild confusion rather than resistance.

checklist.item: Establishes rapport and asks permission before exploring symptoms in detail
Uses a structured symptom history including frequency, duration, and triggers
Screens for alarm symptoms such as dysphagia, weight loss, or gastrointestinal bleeding
Recognizes that symptoms occurring more than twice weekly for over two months exceed the scope of short-term self-care
Recommends referral for possible gastroesophageal reflux disease assessment rather than indefinite antacid use
Suggests appropriate lifestyle modifications as an adjunct
Explains the rationale for referral in terms the patient can understand
Overall organization and clinical judgment

model_answer: A strong candidate begins by exploring the pattern of the patient's heartburn, including frequency, timing relative to meals and lying down, and duration of symptoms. The candidate screens for alarm features such as difficulty swallowing, unintentional weight loss, or signs of bleeding, and confirms none are present. Recognizing that symptoms occurring several times weekly for two months exceed the typical window for short-term self-treatment, the candidate explains that this pattern is consistent with possible gastroesophageal reflux disease and warrants a medical assessment rather than continued unsupervised antacid use. The candidate offers practical lifestyle advice such as avoiding late meals and elevating the head of the bed, and closes by confirming the patient's understanding of the referral plan.

title: Musculoskeletal Pain Complaint Seeking OTC Analgesic
candidate_instructions: A patient asks for a recommendation for lower back pain that started after moving boxes over the weekend. Assess the complaint, screen for red flags, consider any relevant history that could affect analgesic choice, and recommend an appropriate self-care option.
materials_provided: None beyond the instructions above.
sp_role_script: You are a 52 year old man with lower back pain that began two days ago after lifting heavy boxes. The pain is a dull ache across the lower back, worse with bending, without radiation down the legs, numbness, or weakness. You have a history of a stomach ulcer diagnosed three years ago and take a proton pump inhibitor daily. You have no known allergies. Your opening line is asking for something strong for back pain, maybe ibuprofen, since that is what worked for a friend. If asked about the pain pattern, describe it as above. If asked about medical history, mention the ulcer history when prompted, showing surprise that it might matter. If asked about medications, mention the proton pump inhibitor. If steered away from an NSAID, ask why, showing curiosity rather than resistance.

checklist.item: Greets the patient and clarifies the nature and onset of the pain
Asks about red flag symptoms such as leg numbness, weakness, or bowel or bladder changes
Elicits the history of peptic ulcer disease before recommending an analgesic
Identifies that an oral NSAID such as ibuprofen is inappropriate given the ulcer history
Recommends an appropriate alternative such as acetaminophen or a topical NSAID with counselling on gastrointestinal risk
Advises on non-pharmacologic measures such as activity modification and heat
Explains the reasoning for avoiding oral NSAIDs in a way the patient understands
Overall safety-focused clinical reasoning

model_answer: A strong candidate takes a focused pain history and screens for red flags such as radiating pain, numbness, weakness, or bowel or bladder changes, confirming none are present. The candidate then asks about relevant medical history and medications, uncovering the patient's history of peptic ulcer disease and current proton pump inhibitor use. Recognizing that an oral NSAID would increase gastrointestinal risk in this patient, the candidate avoids recommending ibuprofen and instead suggests acetaminophen or a topical NSAID as safer alternatives, explaining the reasoning clearly. The candidate reinforces the recommendation with non-drug measures such as gentle activity and heat application, and advises follow-up if pain worsens or does not improve within a reasonable timeframe.

title: Pediatric Fever Dosing Question from a Parent
setting: A community pharmacy counter, early evening.
candidate_instructions: A parent approaches with her 3 year old child asking how much acetaminophen to give for a fever. Gather the necessary history including the child's weight, symptom duration, and any other relevant details, then provide safe, accurate weight-based dosing guidance and appropriate self-care advice.
materials_provided: A note card stating the child weighs 14 kilograms, available if the candidate asks for the child's weight.
sp_role_script: You are the mother of a 3 year old who has had a fever of 38.9 degrees Celsius since this afternoon, along with a runny nose and mild fussiness but is still drinking fluids and playing at times. You are unsure of the correct dose of children's acetaminophen liquid and worried about giving too much. Your opening line is asking how many milliliters of the children's liquid you should give your child. If asked the child's weight, state it is about 14 kilograms if you already know, or say you are not sure and need to check, prompting the candidate to ask for it directly. If asked about other symptoms, mention the runny nose and fussiness, and confirm the child is still drinking and alert. If asked about other medications, say you have not given anything yet today. Show mild anxiety about dosing accuracy throughout.

checklist.item: Uses a calm, reassuring tone appropriate for an anxious parent
Confirms the child's current weight before calculating a dose
Calculates an accurate weight-based acetaminophen dose using an appropriate mg per kg range
Converts the dose correctly to the volume of the specific concentration product
Screens for red flags such as lethargy, poor fluid intake, difficulty breathing, or persistent high fever
Advises on maximum daily dosing and dosing interval
Provides clear, jargon-free instructions the parent can follow at home
Overall accuracy and safety of dosing counselling

model_answer: A strong candidate reassures the parent, confirms the child's current weight, and asks about symptom duration, hydration status, activity level, and any other medications already given. The candidate screens for red flags such as lethargy, difficulty breathing, or poor fluid intake, confirming the child remains alert and drinking well. Using the confirmed weight, the candidate calculates an accurate acetaminophen dose within the standard mg per kg range and converts it correctly to the appropriate volume of the specific product concentration available. The candidate clearly explains the dosing interval, maximum daily dose, and signs that would warrant seeking further medical care, and confirms the parent's understanding before closing.
references_provided: A pediatric acetaminophen dosing chart is available on request.

title: Suspected Adverse Drug Reaction from a New Prescription
candidate_instructions: A patient calls the pharmacy reporting a persistent dry cough that started about ten days after starting a new blood pressure medication. Take a focused history, identify the likely cause, and resolve the drug therapy problem appropriately, including communicating with the prescriber if needed.
materials_provided: The patient's profile shows ramipril 5 mg daily started twelve days ago for hypertension, with no other new medications.
sp_role_script: You are a 61 year old man who started a new blood pressure pill about twelve days ago. Since about two days after starting it you have had a persistent dry, tickly cough with nothing else, no fever, no shortness of breath, no chest pain. You assumed it was just a cold at first but it has not gone away and is starting to annoy you, especially at night. Your opening line is mentioning you have had this annoying cough for over a week and wondering if you need cough medicine. If asked about timing relative to starting the new medication, connect the dots when prompted. If asked about other symptoms, deny fever, shortness of breath, or chest pain. If the candidate suggests the medication may be the cause, express surprise but willingness to consider switching if the candidate explains clearly.

checklist.item: Takes a systematic history of the cough including onset relative to medication changes
Explains the suspected cause in clear, understandable language
Correctly identifies ACE inhibitor induced cough as the likely drug therapy problem
Screens for red flags such as shortness of breath, chest pain, or signs of angioedema
Recommends an appropriate resolution such as switching to an angiotensin receptor blocker, in consultation with the prescriber
Advises the patient not to stop the medication abruptly without guidance
Demonstrates appropriate collaboration or plan to contact the prescriber
Overall clinical reasoning and patient safety focus

model_answer: A strong candidate takes a detailed history establishing that the dry, non-productive cough began shortly after starting ramipril, with no fever, shortness of breath, or facial swelling. The candidate recognizes this as a classic ACE inhibitor induced cough rather than an infectious cause and explains this connection to the patient in simple terms. The candidate screens for signs of angioedema as a more urgent related concern and confirms none are present. The candidate advises the patient not to stop the medication on their own, and proposes contacting the prescriber to discuss switching to an angiotensin receptor blocker as a class alternative that typically does not cause this cough. The candidate closes by confirming next steps and follow-up.

title: Non-Adherent Patient Missing Diabetes Medication Doses
setting: A community pharmacy consultation area during a routine refill pickup.
candidate_instructions: A patient picking up a metformin refill admits she has been missing doses several times a week. Explore the reasons for missed doses, identify barriers to adherence, and develop a practical plan with the patient to improve adherence.
materials_provided: Refill history shows the last metformin prescription was picked up ten days late, consistent with a pattern over the past three refills.
sp_role_script: You are a 49 year old woman with type 2 diabetes who has been prescribed metformin 500 mg twice daily. You often forget the midday dose because you are at work and it is not part of your routine, and sometimes you skip the evening dose too because you forget once you get home tired. You are not experiencing significant gastrointestinal side effects, just forgetfulness and a busy schedule. You feel a little embarrassed about this. Your opening line is a casual comment that you are here for your refill and hope it is not too big a deal that you are running a bit late again. If asked directly, admit to missing doses several times a week and explain the reasons above. If offered a simplified regimen or reminder strategy, respond positively and with relief rather than resistance.

checklist.item: Approaches the adherence conversation without judgment, normalizing the difficulty
Uses open-ended questions to uncover the specific pattern and reasons for missed doses
Identifies forgetfulness and lack of routine as the primary adherence barriers
Proposes a practical, patient-specific solution such as reminder tools, pillbox, or regimen simplification
Considers whether an extended-release or once-daily formulation could reduce dosing burden
Explains the clinical importance of adherence in terms relevant to the patient's goals
Collaborates with the patient to select a solution she is likely to follow
Overall empathy and problem-solving approach

model_answer: A strong candidate opens the conversation in a non-judgmental way, normalizing that missing doses is common, which helps the patient feel comfortable disclosing the true pattern. The candidate explores the specific barriers and identifies that forgetfulness during a busy workday, rather than side effects, is the main issue. The candidate proposes practical solutions such as a phone reminder, a pillbox, or pairing doses with daily habits, and discusses whether switching to an extended-release once-daily metformin formulation could reduce the burden. The candidate briefly explains why consistent adherence matters for the patient's diabetes control in terms tied to her own goals, and collaborates with her to choose a strategy she feels confident she can maintain, confirming a follow-up plan.

title: Elderly Patient with a Possible Adverse Drug Reaction
setting: A patient's home during a community pharmacist follow-up visit, or a pharmacy consultation room.
candidate_instructions: An elderly patient's daughter reports that her father has become increasingly confused and unsteady on his feet over the past week. Review his recent medication changes, identify a likely drug-related cause, and recommend an appropriate resolution.
materials_provided: Medication profile shows a new prescription for a benzodiazepine for sleep started nine days ago, added to his existing medications for blood pressure and cholesterol.
sp_role_script: You are the daughter of an 82 year old man who started a new sleeping pill about nine days ago. Since then he has seemed more confused than usual, has had two near-falls getting up at night, and seems drowsy during the day. He has not had any new symptoms suggesting infection, no fever, no urinary symptoms. Your opening line is expressing worry that your father seems off since starting his new sleep medication and asking if that could be related. If asked about the timeline, connect it clearly to the new medication. If asked about other symptoms, deny fever or urinary changes. If the candidate suggests the new medication may be responsible, respond with relief that there might be an explanation, and ask what should be done next.

checklist.item: Listens attentively to the daughter's concerns and validates them
Gathers a clear timeline connecting symptom onset to the new medication
Correctly identifies the benzodiazepine as the likely cause of confusion and falls risk in an elderly patient
Screens for alternative causes such as infection or other new symptoms
Recommends discontinuation or dose reduction in consultation with the prescriber, avoiding abrupt unsupervised cessation
Discusses non-pharmacologic alternatives for sleep and fall-prevention precautions
Demonstrates awareness of increased sensitivity to sedatives in elderly patients
Overall safety-focused clinical judgment

model_answer: A strong candidate listens carefully to the daughter's concerns and gathers a clear timeline showing that confusion, unsteadiness, and daytime drowsiness began shortly after the new benzodiazepine was started. The candidate screens briefly for other causes such as infection and confirms none are present. Recognizing that elderly patients are more sensitive to sedative-hypnotics and at increased fall risk, the candidate identifies the new medication as the likely cause of the adverse reaction. The candidate recommends contacting the prescriber to discuss tapering or discontinuing the medication rather than stopping it abruptly, and suggests non-pharmacologic sleep strategies along with fall-prevention precautions in the home. The candidate closes by arranging a follow-up to confirm improvement.

title: Dermatologic Complaint Seeking OTC Treatment
candidate_instructions: A patient asks for a recommendation for an itchy, red rash on his forearm that appeared two days ago after gardening. Assess the rash presentation, screen for red flags, and recommend an appropriate OTC treatment or referral.
materials_provided: None beyond the instructions above.
sp_role_script: You are a 35 year old man who developed an itchy, red, slightly raised rash on his forearm two days ago after spending the weekend gardening and clearing brush. The area is mildly swollen and itchy but not oozing, not spreading rapidly, and you have no fever. You suspect it might be from contact with a plant. Your opening line is asking for something to stop the itching on your arm from a rash you think came from gardening. If asked about appearance and timing, describe it as above. If asked about spreading, fever, or systemic symptoms, deny them. If asked about known allergies, mention none. If offered a topical treatment, respond positively.

checklist.item: Asks about the appearance, location, timing, and likely trigger of the rash
Screens for signs of infection or systemic involvement
Identifies the presentation as consistent with allergic contact dermatitis
Recommends an appropriate topical treatment such as a low to mid potency topical corticosteroid or oral antihistamine for itch
Advises on avoidance of the causative plant and supportive skin care
Identifies red flags that would prompt referral, such as spreading, blistering, fever, or facial or airway involvement
Provides clear counselling on correct application and duration of use
Overall clinical reasoning for a dermatologic self-care complaint

model_answer: A strong candidate asks about the onset, appearance, and likely trigger of the rash, confirming it appeared after gardening and is localized, itchy, and mildly swollen without oozing or spreading. The candidate screens for red flags such as fever, rapid spread, blistering, or facial or airway swelling, confirming none are present. Recognizing the presentation as allergic contact dermatitis, likely from plant exposure, the candidate recommends a short course of a topical corticosteroid or an oral antihistamine for itch relief, with clear instructions on application and duration. The candidate advises avoiding further contact with the suspected plant and covering the area during future gardening, and explains which symptoms would warrant a follow-up visit if the rash worsens.

title: Patient Requesting an OTC Product Contraindicated for Her Condition
candidate_instructions: A patient asks specifically for a pseudoephedrine-containing decongestant for nasal congestion. Explore her history, identify why this product may be inappropriate for her, and recommend a safer alternative.
materials_provided: Patient profile indicates a diagnosis of hypertension currently managed with medication, blood pressure not well controlled at last reading.
sp_role_script: You are a 60 year old woman with high blood pressure that your doctor recently told you is still a bit high despite your medication. You have nasal congestion from a cold and specifically want the pseudoephedrine decongestant tablets you have used in the past because they work well for you. You do not think your blood pressure is relevant to this request. Your opening line is asking for the decongestant tablets by name, stating they have always worked for your stuffy nose. If asked about medical history, mention your blood pressure and that it was recently noted as not well controlled. If the candidate raises a concern about the interaction, express mild surprise since you have used this product before, but be willing to consider an alternative if it is explained clearly.

checklist.item: Asks about relevant medical history before confirming the product request
Explains the concern in clear, non-alarming language
Identifies uncontrolled hypertension as a contraindication to oral pseudoephedrine
Explains the mechanism briefly, that decongestants can raise blood pressure further
Recommends an appropriate alternative such as a saline nasal spray or intranasal corticosteroid, or a topical decongestant with limited duration if suitable
Advises follow-up on blood pressure control with her physician
Respects patient preference while prioritizing safety
Overall clinical judgment and counselling clarity

model_answer: A strong candidate begins by confirming the patient's request but pauses to ask about relevant medical history before agreeing to it, uncovering that her hypertension is currently not well controlled. The candidate explains, in simple terms, that oral pseudoephedrine can raise blood pressure and is not recommended in patients with uncontrolled hypertension. Rather than simply refusing the request, the candidate offers a safer alternative such as a saline nasal spray or an intranasal corticosteroid, and discusses whether a short-term topical decongestant might be reasonable if her physician agrees. The candidate encourages the patient to follow up with her physician regarding her blood pressure control and confirms she understands the reasoning before closing the conversation respectfully.

title: Time-Sensitive Request for Emergency Contraception
setting: A community pharmacy counter, evening shift.
candidate_instructions: A patient approaches the counter quietly and asks about emergency contraception after unprotected intercourse the previous night. Provide timely, confidential, and non-judgmental counselling, confirm the appropriate product and timing, and address any relevant history.
materials_provided: None beyond the instructions above.
sp_role_script: You are a 24 year old woman who had unprotected intercourse last night and is worried about pregnancy. You are not currently on any regular hormonal contraception and have no known drug allergies. Your last menstrual period was about two weeks ago. You feel a bit anxious and embarrassed asking about this in a public pharmacy area. Your opening line is asking quietly if the pharmacist can help you get the morning after pill. If asked about timing, confirm it was about 15 hours ago. If asked about regular contraception or medications, state you take none regularly. If asked about weight, state it if prompted as roughly 70 kilograms. Show relief once the candidate offers a discreet, judgment-free process.

checklist.item: Responds promptly and moves the conversation to a private or discreet setting
Uses a non-judgmental, reassuring tone throughout
Confirms timing since intercourse to determine eligibility and product options
Selects and counsels on an appropriate emergency contraception product for the timeframe
Screens for factors such as body weight that could affect efficacy and discusses options accordingly
Advises on timing of the next menstrual period, what to do if vomiting occurs after taking the dose, and follow-up pregnancy testing if a period is delayed
Discusses ongoing contraception options going forward
Overall efficiency, confidentiality, and professionalism given the time-sensitive nature

model_answer: A strong candidate responds promptly, moves the conversation to a private area, and adopts a calm, non-judgmental tone to ease the patient's visible anxiety. The candidate confirms the timing since unprotected intercourse and takes a brief relevant history including current contraception use, allergies, and body weight, since these can influence product choice and efficacy. The candidate recommends and dispenses an appropriate emergency contraception product for the timeframe, with clear instructions including what to do if vomiting occurs shortly after taking it. The candidate advises the patient on what to expect regarding her next period and to take a pregnancy test if it is delayed, and takes the opportunity to briefly discuss ongoing contraception options for the future, closing the conversation with reassurance and confidentiality maintained throughout.

title: Reporting a Significant Warfarin-Antibiotic Interaction
setting: Community pharmacy, telephone consultation with a family physician's office.
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. A prescription for ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 7 days has just been sent electronically for Mr. Alvarez, age 74, who has been stable on warfarin 5 mg daily for atrial fibrillation with an INR checked two weeks ago at 2.4. You recognize that ciprofloxacin significantly potentiates warfarin and increases bleeding risk. Call the prescribing physician, Dr. Kessler, to discuss the interaction and recommend a safer course of action, including any monitoring or dose adjustment that should occur.
materials_provided: Patient profile showing warfarin 5 mg daily, last INR 2.4 two weeks ago, indication atrial fibrillation, no other anticoagulant or antiplatelet on the profile.
sp_role_script: You are Dr. Kessler, a busy family physician who prescribed ciprofloxacin for a suspected urinary tract infection based on symptoms alone, without ordering a culture. Open the call by saying you are short on time and ask what the pharmacist needs. When the interaction is raised, ask whether a different antibiotic would even be appropriate for a UTI, since you chose ciprofloxacin out of habit. If the candidate proposes an alternative antibiotic with a lower interaction risk (such as nitrofurantoin or a beta-lactam, if renal function and allergy history support it) and recommends an INR recheck within 3 to 5 days, agree to change the order and to have the patient return for INR monitoring. If the candidate does not mention INR monitoring, ask directly whether anything else needs to be done to keep the patient safe. Push back once by saying you have prescribed ciprofloxacin many times before without problems, to see if the candidate defends the recommendation with the pharmacologic rationale rather than backing down.

checklist.item: Introduces self, states purpose of the call clearly, and confirms patient identity
Explains the interaction mechanism in terms the physician can act on without being condescending
Responds to pushback with a clear pharmacologic rationale rather than deferring or abandoning the recommendation
Uses closed-loop communication to confirm the physician's agreed plan before ending the call
Identifies that ciprofloxacin inhibits warfarin metabolism and increases INR and bleeding risk
Recommends a lower-interaction-risk antibiotic alternative appropriate for a urinary tract infection
Recommends INR monitoring within 3 to 5 days of starting any interacting antibiotic
Demonstrates safe, patient-centred clinical judgment consistent with entry-level pharmacist competence

model_answer: A strong candidate identifies that ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2 and CYP3A4 metabolism of warfarin and also disrupts gut flora that produce vitamin K, both of which raise INR and bleeding risk. The candidate proposes an alternative antibiotic such as nitrofurantoin or a beta-lactam after confirming renal function and allergy status, since these carry minimal warfarin interaction. The candidate recommends an INR recheck within 3 to 5 days of starting the new antibiotic and counsels on bleeding signs to watch for in the meantime. When the physician pushes back, the candidate holds the recommendation using the pharmacologic rationale rather than conceding, and closes the call by confirming the physician will send the revised order and the patient will return for INR testing.

title: Questioning an Inappropriate Pediatric Amoxicillin Dose
setting: Community pharmacy, prescription drop-off counter with the prescribing nurse practitioner present in person.
candidate_instructions: A prescription for amoxicillin suspension has been sent for a 3-year-old child, Maya, weighing 14 kg, for acute otitis media, written as amoxicillin 125 mg/5 mL, give 5 mL three times daily. Based on current pediatric dosing guidance for high-dose amoxicillin in otitis media, this dose is well below the recommended range. The prescribing nurse practitioner, NP Delacroix, is at the counter waiting for the prescription. Approach her to clarify the dose and recommend an appropriate adjustment.
materials_provided: Patient profile showing Maya, age 3, weight 14 kg, no known drug allergies, diagnosis of acute otitis media on the prescription.
sp_role_script: You are NP Delacroix, who wrote the prescription quickly between patients and is confident in the dose as written. Open by asking the pharmacist what the concern is, since the dose 'looks standard' to you. When the candidate raises the underdosing concern, ask what dose they would actually recommend and why, expecting a weight-based calculation in mg/kg/day. If the candidate provides a correct calculation using 80 to 90 mg/kg/day divided into two or three doses for high-dose therapy, and translates it into a practical volume of suspension, agree to revise the prescription. Push back once by asking whether the current lower dose might still work and just take longer to clear the infection, to test whether the candidate explains the resistance and treatment failure risk of underdosing rather than agreeing it might be fine.

checklist.item: Approaches the nurse practitioner respectfully and states the concern directly without accusing
Explains the rationale for high-dose amoxicillin in otitis media clearly and confidently
Responds to pushback by addressing resistance and treatment failure risk rather than conceding the lower dose is adequate
Confirms the final agreed dose and next steps before ending the conversation
Calculates weight-based dosing using 80 to 90 mg/kg/day for high-dose amoxicillin
Recommends a specific corrected volume and frequency of the suspension
Identifies risk of resistant Streptococcus pneumoniae as the reason for high-dose therapy in otitis media
Demonstrates safe pediatric dosing judgment consistent with entry-level pharmacist competence

model_answer: A strong candidate calculates that at 14 kg, the prescribed dose of 375 mg/day is far below the recommended 80 to 90 mg/kg/day used for high-dose amoxicillin in otitis media, which would be roughly 1120 to 1260 mg/day divided into two doses. The candidate proposes a corrected regimen, such as approximately 600 mg twice daily, and explains this in practical suspension volume using an appropriate concentration. The candidate explains that underdosing risks treatment failure and selection of resistant Streptococcus pneumoniae, addressing the nurse practitioner's suggestion that a lower dose would simply work more slowly. The candidate confirms the nurse practitioner will revise and resend the prescription before ending the conversation.

title: Clarifying an Ambiguous Insulin Order at Discharge
setting: Hospital pharmacy, discharge medication reconciliation call with the discharging hospitalist.
candidate_instructions: You are reviewing the discharge order for Mr. Okafor, age 68, being sent home after a hospitalization for hyperglycemia. The discharge order reads 'insulin glargine, adjust as needed' with no specified starting dose or frequency, and the inpatient record shows he received varying glargine doses of 18 to 24 units nightly during admission. Call the discharging hospitalist, Dr. Wren, to clarify the exact discharge dose so an accurate prescription can be dispensed and the patient can be counselled safely.
materials_provided: Inpatient MAR excerpt showing glargine doses of 18, 20, 22, and 24 units on different nights over the admission, most recent two nights at 22 units.
sp_role_script: You are Dr. Wren, the hospitalist who is finishing rounds and wrote the order quickly assuming the outpatient team would titrate. Open by saying the dose was 'basically stable around 20-ish units' and that the pharmacist can just pick something reasonable. When the candidate asks for a precise, unambiguous discharge dose, initially suggest simply continuing 'whatever the last dose was' without checking the chart. If the candidate points out that an exact number is needed for a safe, dispensable prescription and suggests using the most recent stable dose (22 units) with a clear follow-up plan for outpatient titration, agree and provide that number. Push back once by asking whether this level of precision really matters for insulin, to test whether the candidate explains why a vague order is unsafe for dispensing and patient self-administration.

checklist.item: States clearly why an exact, unambiguous dose is required before the prescription can be dispensed
