Uses the inpatient MAR data professionally to guide the physician toward a specific number
Responds to pushback by explaining the patient safety risk of a vague insulin order rather than dropping the request
Confirms the exact discharge dose, frequency, and follow-up plan before ending the call
Identifies the most recent stable inpatient dose as the appropriate basis for the discharge order
Recommends a clear outpatient follow-up or titration plan rather than leaving the dose open-ended
Recognizes that an ambiguous insulin order risks both dispensing errors and patient self-administration errors
Demonstrates safe medication reconciliation judgment consistent with entry-level pharmacist competence

model_answer: A strong candidate reviews the inpatient MAR and notes the dose trended toward 22 units nightly over the final two days, then proposes this as the discharge starting dose. The candidate explains to the physician that a vague order such as 'adjust as needed' cannot be safely dispensed or self-administered, since neither the pharmacy nor the patient has a clear target. When the physician suggests simply continuing 'whatever the last dose was' without specifying it, the candidate insists on pinning down the exact number from the chart. The candidate also recommends a specific outpatient follow-up timeframe, such as within one to two weeks, for further titration based on home glucose readings, and confirms this plan with the physician before ending the call.

title: Recommending a Therapeutic Substitution for a Drug Shortage
setting: Community pharmacy, telephone consultation with the prescribing physician regarding a current national shortage.
candidate_instructions: Your pharmacy has been notified of a national shortage of extended-release metoprolol succinate, affecting a regular patient, Mrs. Thibault, age 61, who takes metoprolol succinate 100 mg once daily for hypertension and post-myocardial-infarction cardioprotection. No supply is expected for at least six weeks. Call the prescribing physician, Dr. Nasser, to recommend an appropriate therapeutic substitution that maintains equivalent beta-blockade and cardioprotective effect.
materials_provided: Patient profile confirming metoprolol succinate 100 mg once daily, history of myocardial infarction 8 months ago, no other beta-blocker on file, blood pressure at last visit 128/78.
sp_role_script: You are Dr. Nasser, who is unaware of the shortage and initially assumes the pharmacy will simply find generic metoprolol succinate elsewhere. Open by asking why this needs a phone call rather than just being filled. When the candidate explains the shortage and proposes an alternative, such as switching to an equivalent total daily dose of immediate-release metoprolol tartrate split into two doses, or bisoprolol at an appropriate cardioprotective dose, listen and ask how they arrived at the equivalent dose. If the rationale is sound, agree to the switch. Push back once by asking whether switching formulations risks destabilizing a patient who has been doing well, to test whether the candidate reassures with correct equivalency dosing and a monitoring plan rather than dismissing the concern.

checklist.item: Clearly explains the shortage situation and the reason a substitution is being proposed
Presents the proposed alternative and its dosing rationale in a structured, confident manner
Responds to the physician's concern about destabilization with a specific monitoring plan rather than dismissing it
Confirms the agreed substitution, dose, and monitoring plan before ending the call
Proposes a pharmacologically equivalent alternative beta-blocker regimen at an appropriate cardioprotective dose
Explains the dosing conversion rationale correctly (e.g., succinate to tartrate total daily dose equivalence)
Recommends follow-up blood pressure and heart rate monitoring after the switch
Demonstrates safe, evidence-based substitution judgment consistent with entry-level pharmacist competence

model_answer: A strong candidate explains that metoprolol succinate 100 mg extended-release is unavailable nationally and proposes switching to metoprolol tartrate 50 mg twice daily as an equivalent total daily dose, or alternatively bisoprolol 5 mg once daily, both of which maintain comparable beta-1 blockade for cardioprotection. The candidate explains the conversion rationale clearly, noting that the succinate and tartrate salts are dosed at the same total daily milligram amount despite different release profiles. When the physician expresses concern about destabilizing a stable patient, the candidate reassures with the equivalency data and proposes a blood pressure and heart rate check within one to two weeks after the switch. The candidate confirms the physician agrees to the new prescription and the monitoring plan before ending the call.

title: Discussing a Renal-Function-Triggered Dose Adjustment
setting: Long-term care pharmacy, telephone consultation with the attending nurse practitioner after a routine lab review.
candidate_instructions: While reviewing monthly labs for a long-term care resident, Mr. Bianchi, age 82, on gabapentin 600 mg three times daily for neuropathic pain, you note his most recent creatinine clearance has dropped to 28 mL/min from a baseline of 55 mL/min three months ago. Gabapentin requires dose adjustment as renal function declines, and this dose is now well above the range recommended for his current clearance. Call the attending nurse practitioner, NP Osei, to discuss the lab change and recommend a dose adjustment.
materials_provided: Lab trend showing creatinine clearance declining from 55 to 28 mL/min over three months, current gabapentin dose 600 mg three times daily, no recent dose change on record.
sp_role_script: You are NP Osei, who has not yet reviewed this month's labs due to a heavy caseload. Open by thanking the pharmacist for the flag and asking what adjustment is recommended. If the candidate recommends a reduced total daily dose and adjusted frequency appropriate for a creatinine clearance around 28 mL/min, such as a lower dose given once daily, agree to write the new order. Ask what symptoms of gabapentin toxicity the pharmacy will watch for, expecting sedation, confusion, or ataxia. Push back once by asking whether the dose really needs to change if the patient currently seems fine, to test whether the candidate explains that gabapentin accumulation risk is based on renal clearance regardless of current symptoms, since toxicity can develop gradually.

checklist.item: Presents the lab trend and its clinical significance clearly and proactively
Recommends a specific, appropriately reduced dose and frequency rather than a vague suggestion
Responds to pushback by explaining accumulation risk based on renal clearance rather than current symptom absence
Confirms the plan for monitoring and follow-up before ending the call
Identifies the correct dose range adjustment for gabapentin at a creatinine clearance around 28 mL/min
Names signs of gabapentin toxicity to monitor, such as sedation, confusion, or ataxia
Recommends a follow-up renal function recheck within an appropriate timeframe
Demonstrates safe renal dose-adjustment judgment consistent with entry-level pharmacist competence

model_answer: A strong candidate identifies that a creatinine clearance of 28 mL/min requires a substantial gabapentin dose reduction, proposing a total daily dose in the range of 300 to 600 mg given once daily rather than the current 1800 mg per day given three times daily. The candidate explains that the decline from 55 to 28 mL/min significantly reduces renal clearance of gabapentin, raising accumulation and toxicity risk even though the patient currently appears asymptomatic. The candidate lists sedation, confusion, and ataxia as signs of toxicity the care team should monitor for after the dose change. The candidate confirms the nurse practitioner will write the revised order and schedules a renal function recheck in the coming weeks.

title: Written Drug Information Request: NSAID Use After Cardiac Stenting
setting: Hospital pharmacy drug information service, written response required within the station time.
candidate_instructions: You receive a written drug information request from a cardiology resident. Read the clinical scenario provided and write a clear, structured response addressing the safety of NSAID use in this patient and what alternative approach you would recommend for pain control, with your reasoning.
materials_provided: Written vignette: Mr. Feretti, age 59, had a drug-eluting stent placed 5 weeks ago for an acute coronary syndrome and is on dual antiplatelet therapy with ASA 81 mg daily and ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily. He has ongoing knee osteoarthritis pain and the resident is considering adding ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily as needed. The resident asks whether this is safe and what you would recommend instead.
task_details: The candidate must write a structured response covering: (1) the bleeding risk of combining an NSAID with dual antiplatelet therapy, (2) the cardiovascular risk of NSAIDs in a patient with recent coronary stenting, including any concern about interference with antiplatelet effect, and (3) a specific alternative pain management recommendation with rationale, such as acetaminophen first-line and non-pharmacologic measures, avoiding NSAIDs entirely during the dual antiplatelet therapy period.

checklist.item: Identifies that combining an NSAID with dual antiplatelet therapy substantially increases gastrointestinal and systemic bleeding risk
Identifies that NSAIDs carry an independent cardiovascular risk that is particularly concerning within the first year after coronary stenting
Recommends against adding ibuprofen while the patient is on dual antiplatelet therapy
Recommends an appropriate alternative, such as acetaminophen as first-line therapy with attention to maximum daily dose
Mentions non-pharmacologic or adjunct measures for osteoarthritis pain as part of a complete answer
Response is clearly organized, clinically accurate, and directly answers both parts of the resident's question
Demonstrates safe, evidence-based clinical judgment consistent with entry-level pharmacist competence

model_answer: A strong written response explains that adding ibuprofen to ASA and ticagrelor would create triple antithrombotic-type bleeding risk, substantially raising the chance of gastrointestinal and other major bleeding. It notes that NSAIDs also carry an independent cardiovascular risk, including a possible prothrombotic effect, which is especially concerning within the first year after drug-eluting stent placement when the risk of stent thrombosis remains elevated. The response recommends avoiding ibuprofen and any NSAID for the duration of dual antiplatelet therapy, proposing acetaminophen up to the recommended maximum daily dose as first-line pain control. It also suggests non-pharmacologic measures such as physiotherapy, weight management if applicable, and topical analgesics as adjuncts, and recommends reassessing NSAID use only after dual antiplatelet therapy has concluded and in consultation with cardiology.

title: Written Drug Information Request: Methotrexate and Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole Interaction
setting: Community pharmacy drug information request from a walk-in patient's family member, written response required within the station time.
candidate_instructions: Read the clinical scenario provided and write a clear, structured response addressing the safety concern raised and the specific action you recommend, with your reasoning, for the pharmacy to take before this prescription is filled.
materials_provided: Written vignette: Mrs. Halvorsen, age 70, takes methotrexate 15 mg once weekly for rheumatoid arthritis along with folic acid 5 mg once weekly. She has just been prescribed trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS twice daily for 10 days for a suspected urinary tract infection by an urgent care physician who did not have access to her regular medication list.
task_details: The candidate must write a structured response covering: (1) the mechanism and severity of the methotrexate and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole interaction, including the risk of pancytopenia, (2) why this combination should generally be avoided rather than just monitored, and (3) a specific recommended action, such as contacting the prescriber to request an alternative antibiotic and explaining what alternatives would be appropriate.

checklist.item: Identifies that trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole inhibits folate metabolism in a manner that compounds methotrexate's antifolate effect
Identifies the risk of severe, potentially life-threatening pancytopenia or bone marrow suppression from this combination
Recommends against dispensing the combination as prescribed and recommends contacting the prescriber
Suggests an appropriate alternative antibiotic for the urinary tract infection that avoids this interaction
Notes the interaction risk applies even with concurrent folic acid supplementation
Response is clearly organized, clinically accurate, and directly answers what action the pharmacy should take
Demonstrates safe, evidence-based clinical judgment consistent with entry-level pharmacist competence

model_answer: A strong written response explains that trimethoprim inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, adding to methotrexate's own antifolate mechanism, and that this combination has been associated with severe, sometimes fatal pancytopenia and bone marrow suppression, particularly in older adults. It notes that folic acid supplementation does not reliably prevent this interaction, so the combination should be avoided rather than simply monitored more closely. The response recommends holding the prescription and contacting the urgent care prescriber to explain the patient's methotrexate therapy, which was not visible without her full medication history. It proposes an alternative antibiotic without this interaction, such as nitrofurantoin (if renal function permits) or an appropriate beta-lactam, for treatment of the urinary tract infection.

title: Written Drug Information Request: QT-Prolonging Combination in a Post-Discharge Patient
setting: Hospital discharge pharmacy drug information consult, written response required within the station time.
candidate_instructions: Read the clinical scenario provided and write a clear, structured response assessing the safety of the discharge regimen and the specific recommendation you would make to the discharge team, with your reasoning.
materials_provided: Written vignette: Mr. Deschamps, age 77, is being discharged on citalopram 40 mg daily (started 2 years ago for depression; note this exceeds the 20 mg/day maximum recommended for patients over 60 due to dose-dependent QT prolongation risk) and is newly prescribed azithromycin 500 mg on day one followed by 250 mg daily for 4 more days for community-acquired pneumonia, plus ondansetron 4 mg as needed for nausea. His most recent discharge ECG shows a QTc of 470 ms, and his serum potassium is 3.3 mmol/L.
task_details: The candidate must write a structured response covering: (1) identification of all three QT-prolonging agents plus the hypokalemia as compounding risk factors, including that the citalopram dose itself already exceeds the recommended maximum for his age, (2) the specific arrhythmia risk (torsades de pointes) created by this combination, and (3) concrete recommendations, such as correcting the potassium, considering an alternative antibiotic or antiemetic with lower QT risk, and whether an ECG recheck is warranted.

checklist.item: Identifies citalopram, azithromycin, and ondansetron as all being QT-prolonging agents used concurrently
Recognizes that 40 mg daily citalopram exceeds the 20 mg/day maximum recommended for patients over 60 due to dose-dependent QT prolongation risk, independent of the new antibiotic and antiemetic
Identifies the baseline QTc of 470 ms and serum potassium of 3.3 mmol/L as significant compounding risk factors
Explains the risk of torsades de pointes from the combined effect of these factors
Recommends correcting the potassium level before or during this regimen
Recommends a specific medication change, such as an alternative antibiotic or antiemetic with lower QT-prolongation risk
Response is clearly organized, clinically accurate, and directly answers what the discharge team should do
Demonstrates safe, evidence-based clinical judgment consistent with entry-level pharmacist competence

model_answer: A strong written response identifies that citalopram, azithromycin, and ondansetron are each independently associated with QT prolongation, and that using all three together in a patient with an already prolonged baseline QTc of 470 ms and hypokalemia of 3.3 mmol/L creates a meaningful risk of torsades de pointes. It also recognizes that his citalopram dose of 40 mg daily already exceeds the 20 mg/day maximum recommended for patients over 60 due to dose-dependent QT prolongation risk, an additional compounding factor independent of the new antibiotic and antiemetic. It recommends correcting the potassium to at least 4.0 mmol/L before or concurrently with this regimen. It proposes alternatives to reduce risk, such as switching azithromycin to an antibiotic with less QT effect (for example, an appropriate beta-lactam if clinically suitable for the pneumonia) and using a non-QT-prolonging antiemetic such as metoclopramide instead of ondansetron if nausea control is still needed. The response also recommends a repeat ECG to confirm the QTc trend before or shortly after discharge given these compounding risk factors.

title: New Prescription Review: Pediatric Antibiotic Dose
setting: A community pharmacy technician has placed a new prescription on the counter for the candidate to review before it is entered and filled.
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. A new prescription has just come in for a pediatric patient. Review the prescription and the patient profile provided. You have 2 minutes to identify any problems with this order before deciding what action to take. You will not speak with anyone during this station. Write your findings and your planned actions on the response sheet provided.
materials_provided: Prescription for Amoxicillin suspension 400mg/5mL, sig: give 5mL three times daily for 10 days, for a patient listed as 14kg, 3 years old, no known allergies. Patient profile shows a prior reaction of hives and facial swelling to amoxicillin documented 8 months ago. The prescriber's fax signature line and license number are present and legible. The calculated dose from the sig works out to roughly 86mg/kg/day, which is within a normal high-dose otitis media range for weight if allergy were not a factor.
task_details: Identify all problems present in this prescription and the patient profile, and state the specific action you would take for each problem before this prescription could be filled.

checklist.item: Identifies the documented penicillin-class allergy history (hives, facial swelling) as a contraindication to amoxicillin
States that the prescription should not be filled as written due to the allergy conflict
States the correct next action is to contact the prescriber to discuss the allergy and request an alternative agent
Recognizes that the dose itself, taken in isolation, is within an acceptable weight-based range and does not misidentify the dose as the primary error
Does not proceed to enter or fill the prescription without resolving the allergy conflict first
Response is organized, legible, and clearly separates each identified problem from its corresponding action
Demonstrates a systematic approach to prescription screening rather than checking only one element
Uses the full reading period effectively and provides a complete response within the time allowed

model_answer: The planted error is a drug-allergy conflict: the patient has a documented prior reaction of hives and facial swelling to amoxicillin, and the new prescription orders amoxicillin suspension. The correct action is to hold the prescription and contact the prescriber to flag the allergy before any product is prepared or dispensed. The dose itself, at roughly 86mg per kg per day divided three times daily, is within a plausible high-dose range for a weight-based pediatric regimen and is not the primary issue. The candidate should recommend the prescriber select an alternative antibiotic class appropriate for the indication and document the allergy discussion in the patient record.

title: New Prescription Review: Incomplete Controlled Substance Order
setting: A new prescription for a controlled substance has been dropped off at the pharmacy and needs to be screened before entry.
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. Review the attached prescription for hydromorphone tablets before it is processed. You have 2 minutes to identify whether this prescription meets the requirements to be filled as a controlled substance in Canada. You will not speak with anyone during this station. Write your findings and the specific action you would take on the response sheet provided.
materials_provided: Prescription for Hydromorphone 2mg tablets, sig: take 1 tablet every 4 to 6 hours as needed for pain, quantity 60, for an adult patient with a documented history of chronic low back pain. The prescription is handwritten, dated, includes the patient's full name and address, and includes the prescriber's printed name and signature, but has no prescriber identification number and no quantity written in words in addition to the numeral. No indication is given that this is a refill authorization.
task_details: Determine whether this controlled substance prescription meets the requirements to be filled as written. Identify any missing elements and state the action you would take.

checklist.item: Identifies that the prescriber identification number is missing
Identifies that the quantity is not also written in words as required for a controlled substance prescription
States that the prescription cannot be filled until the missing elements are obtained or confirmed with the prescriber
States the correct action is to contact the prescriber's office to verify the missing information before dispensing
Does not propose filling the prescription as an emergency supply when the situation does not meet emergency-supply criteria
Response demonstrates knowledge of the specific written requirements for controlled substance prescriptions
Clearly distinguishes between missing administrative elements and clinical appropriateness
Response is complete, organized, and provided within the allotted time

model_answer: The planted errors are administrative rather than clinical: the prescription is missing the prescriber's identification number and the quantity is not written in words alongside the numeral, both of which are standard requirements for a valid controlled substance prescription in Canada. The correct action is to contact the prescriber's office directly to confirm the identification number and the intended quantity before the prescription is entered or prepared. The candidate should not simply add the missing information from memory or assumption, and should not treat this as an emergency supply situation since there is no indication the patient is without their medication. Once verified directly with the prescriber and documented, the prescription may proceed to normal processing.

title: Final Check: Wrong Strength Dispensed
setting: A prescription has already been filled and labeled by a technician and is presented to the candidate for the final accuracy check before release to the patient.
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist performing the final check on a filled prescription. Compare the original prescription order to the filled vial and label provided. You have 2 minutes to identify any discrepancies between what was ordered and what was prepared. You will not speak with anyone during this station. Write your findings and the corrective action you would take on the response sheet provided.
materials_provided: Original prescription: Metoprolol tartrate 25mg tablets, sig: take 1 tablet twice daily, quantity 60. Filled vial and label show Metoprolol tartrate 50mg tablets, quantity 60, with the sig on the label correctly transcribed as 'take 1 tablet twice daily.' The label's patient name, prescriber name, and drug name are all correct and match the original order exactly, only the tablet strength dispensed does not match.
task_details: Compare the filled prescription to the original order and identify any discrepancy. State what corrective action must be taken before this prescription can be released to the patient.

checklist.item: Identifies that the dispensed strength (50mg) does not match the ordered strength (25mg)
Recognizes that dispensing the wrong strength would result in the patient receiving double the intended dose
States that the prescription must not be released to the patient in its current state
States the correct action is to pull the product, return it to stock, and refill with the correct 25mg strength before release
Recognizes the need to document or flag the error per the pharmacy's error-reporting process
Demonstrates a systematic side-by-side comparison of every element on the order versus the label and product
Correctly identifies that the label transcription itself was accurate and the error occurred at the filling step
Response is complete, organized, and provided within the allotted time

model_answer: The planted error is a wrong-strength dispensing error: the original order calls for metoprolol tartrate 25mg tablets, but the vial was filled with the 50mg strength, which would give the patient double the intended dose. The label transcription itself is accurate and matches the original order in every other respect, so the error occurred during product selection or filling rather than data entry. The correct action is to stop the prescription from being released, pull the incorrect product, return it to stock, and refill the prescription with the correct 25mg tablets. The candidate should also flag this as a near-miss or dispensing error through the pharmacy's internal quality process so the cause can be reviewed.

title: Final Check: Look-Alike Sound-Alike Mix-Up
setting: A filled prescription is presented to the candidate for the final accuracy check, involving two drug names that are commonly confused.
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist performing the final check on a filled prescription. Compare the original prescription order to the filled vial and label provided. You have 2 minutes to identify any discrepancies. You will not speak with anyone during this station. Write your findings and the corrective action you would take on the response sheet provided.
materials_provided: Original prescription: Hydroxyzine 25mg tablets, sig: take 1 tablet at bedtime as needed for itching, quantity 30. Filled vial and label show Hydralazine 25mg tablets, quantity 30, with the label sig transcribed as 'take 1 tablet at bedtime as needed for itching.' The patient name, prescriber, and quantity all match the original order; only the drug actually placed in the vial does not match what was ordered.
task_details: Compare the filled prescription to the original order and identify any discrepancy. State what corrective action must be taken before this prescription can be released to the patient.

checklist.item: Identifies that hydralazine was dispensed in place of the ordered hydroxyzine
Recognizes this as a known look-alike sound-alike pair and names the clinical risk (an antihypertensive given instead of an antihistamine, risking hypotension and unmanaged itching)
States that the prescription must not be released to the patient in its current state
States the correct action is to pull the incorrect product, return it to stock, and refill with the correct hydroxyzine tablets before release
Recognizes the need to document the error and consider a system safeguard such as shelf separation or tall-man lettering for this drug pair
Demonstrates a systematic side-by-side comparison of drug name, strength, sig, and quantity rather than checking only the label print
Correctly recognizes the label transcription itself was accurate and the error occurred at product selection
Response is complete, organized, and provided within the allotted time

model_answer: The planted error is a look-alike sound-alike mix-up: hydralazine 25mg tablets were dispensed instead of the ordered hydroxyzine 25mg tablets, two drug names that are frequently confused due to similar spelling and sound. This is a serious error, since hydralazine is an antihypertensive that could cause unwanted hypotension, while the patient's itching would go untreated. The correct action is to stop the prescription from being released, pull the incorrect product, return it to stock, and refill with the correct hydroxyzine tablets. The candidate should document the error through the pharmacy's quality process and note that this drug pair may need a system safeguard such as physical shelf separation or tall-man lettering to prevent recurrence.

title: Patient Encounter: Insurance-Driven Generic Substitution
setting: A patient has arrived at the pharmacy counter to pick up a refill and is upset to find the product looks different from what they usually receive.
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. A patient is at the counter to pick up a refill of their usual medication and has noticed the pills look different from before. Speak with the patient, explain the reason for the change, address their concerns, and confirm they are comfortable proceeding before the prescription is released. You have 7 minutes with the patient.
sp_role_script: You are a 58-year-old patient picking up your regular refill of atorvastatin 20mg, which you have taken for two years without any problems. Today the tablets in the bag look different in shape and color from your usual ones. You are worried the pharmacy made a mistake or gave you the wrong drug. Opening line: 'These pills look different than what I usually get, did you give me the wrong medication?' If the pharmacist explains this is a different manufacturer of the same generic drug due to your insurance plan's preferred supplier list or a supply change at the pharmacy, listen carefully but ask a follow-up question such as 'Is it definitely the exact same medication, just a different brand?' or 'Will this work the same way as before?' If the pharmacist explains clearly, confirms the active ingredient and strength are identical, and offers to answer further questions or monitor for any change in how you feel, become reassured and agree to accept the refill. If the pharmacist is dismissive, does not explain the reason for the change, or does not check the active ingredient and strength with you, remain worried and ask to speak with someone else or ask for the old product to be found instead.

checklist.item: Confirms the active ingredient, strength, and dosage form are unchanged from the patient's previous fill
Explains the reason for the appearance change in terms the patient can understand (different manufacturer, insurance or supply-driven substitution)
Reassures the patient that this substitution does not change how the medication works
Invites the patient to contact the pharmacy if they notice any change in effect or have further concerns
Obtains the patient's comfort and agreement before releasing the prescription
Uses plain, non-technical language appropriate for the patient
Listens to the patient's concern fully before responding
Maintains a calm, reassuring, and professional tone throughout the encounter

title: Patient Encounter: Early Refill Request for a Controlled Substance
setting: A patient approaches the pharmacy counter asking to pick up their pain medication several days earlier than the refill would normally be due.
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist on duty. A patient at the counter is asking to refill their controlled substance prescription several days before it is due. Speak with the patient to understand the reason for the request, explain what can and cannot be done, and discuss next steps. You have 7 minutes with the patient.
sp_role_script: You are a 45-year-old patient who takes hydromorphone for chronic pain following a back injury. Your prescription record shows it is not due for refill for another 6 days, but you are asking to fill it today because you say you are 'running low' and have an upcoming weekend trip. You do not have documentation of lost or stolen medication, and there is no indication of an early hospital discharge or dose change. Opening line: 'I know it's a bit early, but can you just fill my pain medication today? I'm going away this weekend.' If the pharmacist explains that controlled substances cannot be filled early without a valid clinical reason or prescriber authorization, react with mild frustration but ask what your options are. If the pharmacist offers to contact the prescriber to discuss the request, or offers to check your fill history for other solutions, calm down and agree to that plan. If the pharmacist is rigid, refuses to engage further, or does not offer any next step, become more frustrated and ask to speak to the pharmacy manager.

checklist.item: Explains clearly that the medication cannot be filled early without a valid clinical reason or prescriber authorization
Asks open-ended questions to understand the reason behind the early request (running low, travel, lost dose, changed regimen)
Reviews or offers to review the patient's fill history to check for a pattern or documented cause
Offers to contact the prescriber to discuss the request rather than refusing outright with no next step
Does not agree to dispense the controlled substance early without proper authorization
Communicates the refusal without sounding accusatory or judgmental toward the patient
Acknowledges the patient's stated concern (upcoming travel) while still holding the appropriate boundary
Maintains a calm, professional, and non-confrontational tone throughout the encounter

title: Justifying Continued Metformin Monotherapy Instead of Adding a GLP-1 Agonist
setting: Family health team office, shared with a physician who wants a quick curbside consult before her next patient
candidate_instructions: You are the pharmacist embedded in this clinic. Dr. Osei wants to discuss a 58-year-old patient with type 2 diabetes, A1C 7.2 percent on maximum metformin, normal renal function, and no established cardiovascular or kidney disease. She read that GLP-1 agonists should be added now. Review the chart summary provided and discuss with her whether escalation is warranted at this time, using current Diabetes Canada guidance on treatment intensification and cardiovascular risk stratification.
materials_provided: One page chart summary: A1C trend over 3 visits (7.6, 7.4, 7.2), fasting glucose log, no ASCVD or CKD, BMI 27, patient reports good adherence and no hypoglycemia
sp_role_script: You are Dr. Osei, a busy but collegial family physician. Open with, I saw a drug rep about a new GLP-1 last week, should we just add it now since her A1C isn't at target yet. If the candidate explains that A1C 7.2 is close to the individualized target and trending down, and that GLP-1 or SGLT2 escalation is prioritized specifically for patients with established cardiovascular disease, chronic kidney disease, or high cardiovascular risk rather than for glycemic control alone in a lower risk patient, respond with interest and ask what you would recommend instead. If the candidate suggests reinforcing lifestyle measures and rechecking A1C in 3 months before adding a second agent, agree. If the candidate immediately agrees to add the GLP-1 without discussing risk stratification or individualized targets, push back once by asking isn't lower A1C always better, and see if they correct themselves. If the candidate cannot explain the guideline-based reasoning at all, express mild frustration and say you will just prescribe it yourself.

checklist.item: Introduces self and confirms the clinical question being asked
Uses clear, organized reasoning when presenting the recommendation to the physician
Responds to physician pushback with evidence rather than deferring immediately
Correctly identifies that A1C 7.2 percent is near an individualized target and trending favorably
Correctly states that GLP-1 or SGLT2 escalation is prioritized for established ASCVD, CKD, or high cardiovascular risk, not glycemic control alone
Recommends a reasonable interim plan (reinforce lifestyle, recheck A1C in a defined interval) rather than an unnecessary agent
Does not recommend escalation solely because A1C is above the general population target of 7 percent
Demonstrates evidence-informed clinical judgment applied to this specific patient's risk profile

model_answer: This patient's A1C of 7.2 percent is close to an individualized target and has been improving over three visits, and she has no established cardiovascular disease, chronic kidney disease, or heart failure. Diabetes Canada guidance reserves early GLP-1 or SGLT2 escalation for patients with these higher risk features because the cardiorenal benefit, not glycemic lowering alone, drives that recommendation. For a lower risk patient still trending toward target on metformin, adding a second agent now offers marginal glycemic benefit while introducing additional cost and side effect burden. A reasonable plan is to reinforce lifestyle counselling and recheck A1C in three months before considering escalation. If A1C plateaus above target or new cardiovascular risk factors emerge, the recommendation would change accordingly.
references_provided: Diabetes Canada 2023 clinical practice guideline chapter on pharmacologic glycemic management, one page excerpt on risk-based escalation

title: Off-Label Low-Dose Naltrexone Request from a Palliative Care Physician
setting: Hospice inpatient unit, pharmacist consulted at bedside rounds
candidate_instructions: Dr. Whitfield is considering low-dose naltrexone for a patient with refractory cancer-related fatigue after standard options have been tried. He asks for your input on the evidence and whether you support the plan. Review the brief evidence summary provided and discuss the strength of evidence, plausible risks including interaction with the patient's current opioid regimen, and a reasonable monitoring plan if the trial proceeds.
materials_provided: One paragraph evidence summary noting small trials and case series only, no large randomized trials for cancer fatigue, mechanism theories only, current medication list showing scheduled hydromorphone for pain
sp_role_script: You are Dr. Whitfield, a palliative care physician. Say, I've read some small studies suggesting low-dose naltrexone helps with cancer fatigue, and I want to try it for this patient, what do you think. If the candidate correctly notes the evidence is limited to small trials and case series without large randomized data, acknowledge that but ask whether it's still reasonable to try given few other options. If the candidate raises the interaction with the patient's scheduled opioid, since naltrexone is an opioid antagonist and could precipitate withdrawal or blunt analgesia even at low doses, respond with concern and ask what you should do. If the candidate proposes either avoiding it given the opioid conflict or, alternatively, a very cautious monitored trial with the palliative team fully aware of withdrawal risk, accept the reasoning. If the candidate approves the plan without ever mentioning the opioid interaction, interrupt and ask specifically about interactions with her hydromorphone.

checklist.item: Explains evidence quality in plain clinical terms appropriate for physician discussion
Raises the opioid interaction proactively rather than only when prompted
Correctly characterizes the evidence base as limited (small trials, case series, no large RCTs)
Identifies that naltrexone is an opioid antagonist and can precipitate withdrawal or reduce analgesic efficacy in a patient on scheduled opioids
Weighs limited evidence against the significant interaction risk rather than endorsing the plan uncritically
Proposes a clinically sound path forward, either avoiding the combination or a cautious closely monitored approach
Demonstrates appropriate risk-benefit reasoning for an off-label therapy in a complex patient

model_answer: The evidence for low-dose naltrexone in cancer-related fatigue comes only from small trials and case series, with no large randomized controlled trials to confirm efficacy or establish a reliable dosing strategy. More importantly, this patient is on scheduled hydromorphone, and naltrexone is a full opioid antagonist that can precipitate withdrawal symptoms and significantly reduce analgesic control even at low reported doses. Given the weak evidence base and the meaningful risk of destabilizing her pain management, I would not recommend adding it in its current regimen. If the team still wants to pursue it after discussing risks with the patient, it should only be done with a clear plan to hold or substantially adjust the opioid, close monitoring for withdrawal and breakthrough pain, and an agreed stopping point if it worsens comfort.
references_provided: One paragraph literature summary (small trials, case series, no large RCTs) plus current medication administration record

title: Explaining Why Watchful Waiting Is Appropriate for a Child's Ear Infection
candidate_instructions: A parent brings her 4-year-old son's prescription from an urgent care clinic for amoxicillin for acute otitis media, but the visit note documents mild symptoms for less than 24 hours and no fever above 39. She is anxious and wants to know why the clinician mentioned watchful waiting might have been an option. Explain in plain language why observation without immediate antibiotics is a reasonable and evidence-supported approach in mild cases, and address her concerns.
materials_provided: Prescription for amoxicillin suspension, clinic note stating mild otalgia less than 24 hours, temperature 38.2, non-severe symptoms, safety-net advice already given to return if worse
sp_role_script: You are the child's mother, worried and slightly frustrated. Start with, the doctor said something about waiting instead of antibiotics but then still gave us this prescription, should I just fill it or not. Ask why antibiotics wouldn't just fix it faster. If the candidate explains in plain language that many mild ear infections, especially in this age group, improve on their own within a couple of days because the body's immune system often clears them without help, and that antibiotics carry side effects like diarrhea and do not meaningfully speed recovery in mild cases, express relief but ask what you should watch for. If the candidate reassures you the prescription can still be filled and kept on hand to start if things get worse in the next 24 to 48 hours, without pressure to use it immediately, accept this. If the candidate cannot give you a clear reason to wait or uses technical terms like watchful waiting without explaining what it means, ask them to explain it more simply.

checklist.item: Uses plain, non-technical language throughout, avoiding unexplained jargon
Acknowledges the parent's worry before explaining the reasoning
Checks the parent's understanding before ending the conversation
Correctly explains that mild acute otitis media often resolves without antibiotics, particularly with short duration and low grade fever
Correctly explains that antibiotics do not meaningfully speed recovery in mild cases and carry side effect risk
Provides clear safety net instructions (when to start the antibiotic, when to seek further care)
Does not dismiss the parent's concern or pressure her into either filling or not filling the prescription
Successfully translates evidence-based watchful waiting guidance into reassuring, actionable advice for a caregiver

model_answer: I can hear how worried you are, and that makes complete sense. For mild ear infections like this one, where symptoms have only been present a short time and the fever isn't very high, research shows the body's own immune system clears the infection on its own within a couple of days in most children this age. Giving antibiotics right away doesn't usually make the child feel better any faster, and it does come with its own downsides like stomach upset or diarrhea. A good middle ground is to hold onto this prescription, give pain and fever relief as needed, and only start the antibiotic if he isn't improving or gets worse within the next day or two. If he develops a high fever, seems very unwell, or the ear pain gets significantly worse, that would be the signal to start it or come back for reassessment right away.

title: Explaining Why a Newer, More Expensive Anticoagulant Is Not Necessary
candidate_instructions: A patient recently started on warfarin for atrial fibrillation saw an advertisement for a direct oral anticoagulant and wants to switch, believing it must be better since it is newer and does not require blood test monitoring. Review his profile and discuss with him whether switching is medically necessary in his situation, addressing both the clinical reasoning and his out of pocket cost concerns since his private plan does not cover the newer agent well.
materials_provided: Patient profile: stable INR readings in range for the past 4 months, no history of bleeding, private insurance covers warfarin fully but DOAC would cost him significant monthly copay, normal renal function
sp_role_script: You are a 68-year-old man recently started on warfarin. Open with, I saw a commercial for one of those newer blood thinners, no needles or blood tests needed, I think I should switch. Ask isn't newer always better. If the candidate explains that direct oral anticoagulants are a reasonable option for many patients but are not automatically superior for someone already stable and well controlled on warfarin, and that his INR has been consistently in range with no bleeding issues, ask about the cost since you heard warfarin is cheaper. If the candidate confirms his plan covers warfarin fully but the DOAC would mean a real ongoing cost to him for a drug that would offer similar stroke protection in his specific stable case, thank them and say you feel better staying on warfarin. If the candidate pressures you either to switch unnecessarily or dismisses your question without a clear explanation, express confusion and ask again why newer wouldn't just be better.

checklist.item: Uses plain language, avoiding unexplained pharmacologic jargon
Validates the patient's interest in the newer option before explaining why it isn't necessary for him
Correctly explains that direct oral anticoagulants are not universally superior, particularly for a patient already stable and in range on warfarin
Correctly notes his current INR control and absence of bleeding history as reasons continuing warfarin is reasonable
Addresses the real cost difference to the patient given his insurance coverage
Does not claim the newer agent is unsafe or inferior in general, only that it is not necessary for his specific stable situation
Successfully reframes newer as not automatically better in a way the patient understands and accepts

model_answer: It makes sense that the idea of no needles or blood tests sounds appealing, and those newer medications are a good option for many people. But for someone like you who has been steady and well controlled on warfarin for months with no bleeding problems, switching wouldn't actually lower your stroke risk any further, since both types of medication work well when properly managed. On top of that, your insurance covers warfarin completely, while the newer medication would come with a real ongoing cost to you each month for a benefit that, in your specific case, isn't any greater. Given how well things are going, staying on warfarin with your regular monitoring is a perfectly sound choice, and we can always revisit this if your situation changes down the road.

title: Written Comparison of Apixaban and Rivaroxaban for a Specific Patient
candidate_instructions: A nurse practitioner colleague has sent a message asking you to compare apixaban and rivaroxaban for an 80-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation, weight 52 kg, serum creatinine 128 micromol per litre, and a recent minor GI bleed on a prior antiplatelet that has since resolved. Using the brief evidence summary provided, write a structured response comparing the two agents for this specific patient and give a clear recommendation with rationale.
materials_provided: One page evidence summary noting apixaban dose reduction criteria (age, weight, creatinine), rivaroxaban single daily dosing with food, comparative bleeding risk data trends, renal dosing considerations for both agents in this creatinine range
task_details: Candidate has 10 minutes to write a structured clinical response (150 to 250 words) addressing: dose adjustment considerations for each agent given this patient's age, weight, and renal function; relative bleeding risk considerations given her recent GI bleed history; a clear final recommendation with rationale; and any monitoring or follow up point worth flagging to the nurse practitioner. Response should be written as though sent directly to the requesting colleague.

checklist.item: Correctly identifies that this patient meets criteria for apixaban dose reduction based on age, weight, and renal function
Correctly addresses renal dosing considerations for rivaroxaban in this creatinine range
Appropriately weighs the recent GI bleed history in the comparative bleeding risk discussion
Gives one clear final recommendation rather than an ambiguous either-or answer
Rationale logically follows from the evidence summary provided rather than unsupported claims
Flags a reasonable monitoring or follow up point (e.g. renal function recheck, bleeding signs)
Response is concise, well organized, and appropriately professional in tone for a colleague-to-colleague clinical message
Stays within the requested word range and directly answers what was asked

model_answer: Given her age of 80 and weight of 52 kilograms, this patient meets two of the three apixaban dose-reduction criteria (age 80 or older, weight 60 kilograms or under, and creatinine 133 micromol per litre or higher), so a reduced dose of apixaban is appropriate. Her calculated creatinine clearance is approximately 25 mL per minute, which falls within rivaroxaban's 15 to 50 mL per minute renal impairment range, so rivaroxaban would need to be reduced to 15 mg once daily with food if it were chosen, without apixaban's built in dose reduction pathway for this specific combination of factors. Given her recent GI bleed history, apixaban's generally favorable bleeding profile in comparative data makes it the more reasonable choice here. I would recommend apixaban at the reduced dose, with a renal function recheck in three to six months and clear counselling on bleeding warning signs given her prior history.
references_provided: One page evidence summary (dose reduction criteria, renal dosing thresholds, comparative bleeding trends)

title: Angry Patient Disputing an Insurance Charge
setting: Community pharmacy front counter, mid-afternoon, other customers waiting nearby
candidate_instructions: A patient is at the counter upset because their usual maintenance medication is being charged at full price today instead of the small copay they normally pay. You have 7 minutes to speak with the patient, understand the concern, and address it professionally. You do not need to resolve the insurance system itself, but you must communicate clearly and de-escalate the situation.
materials_provided: A printed receipt showing the medication was billed at the cash price because the insurance plan shows as inactive today.
sp_role_script: You are Frank Doyle, 58, picking up your monthly blood pressure medication. You have filled this prescription here for three years and always paid a 10 dollar copay. Today the cashier told you it costs 94 dollars. Open with visible frustration: raise your voice slightly and say you are sick of being nickel and dimed and that this pharmacy is always messing something up. If the pharmacist stays calm and acknowledges your frustration, gradually calm down over the conversation. If the pharmacist becomes defensive, argues, or tries to blame the insurance company without addressing your frustration first, stay agitated and repeat that you want to speak to a manager. Mention partway through that you are on a fixed income and this matters to you. Your emotional cue is anger rooted in financial anxiety, not truly at the pharmacist personally.

checklist.item: Acknowledges the patient's frustration and financial concern before explaining the billing issue
Uses a calm, non-defensive tone throughout the interaction
Avoids interrupting the patient while they are expressing frustration
Uses plain language to explain that the insurance appears inactive rather than blaming the patient or insurer
Offers a concrete next step, such as calling the insurance plan or holding the medication while the issue is investigated
Patient leaves the interaction feeling heard, even if the billing issue is not fully resolved on the spot

model_answer: A strong candidate opens by lowering their own voice and pace, and immediately validates the patient's frustration with a reflective statement such as noting that a sudden price change after years of a stable copay is understandably upsetting. Only after the emotion is acknowledged does the candidate explain, in plain terms, that the system shows the insurance as inactive today rather than assuming the patient did anything wrong. The candidate avoids arguing about whose fault the glitch is and instead offers a concrete path forward, such as calling the insurer together or holding the medication while it is sorted out. Throughout, the candidate uses open body language, avoids jargon like adjudication or rejection codes, and checks in on the patient's fixed income comment with empathy rather than ignoring it.

title: Family Member Picking Up a Deceased Relative's Prescription
candidate_instructions: A person has come to pick up a prescription that was ready for their parent, who passed away three days ago. You have 7 minutes to handle this sensitively, confirm what should happen with the prescription, and support the family member through a difficult moment.
materials_provided: A profile note indicating the prescription was filled five days ago and has not yet been picked up.
sp_role_script: You are Maria Costa, 41, arriving to collect a prescription that was ready for your father, who died unexpectedly three days ago. You are quiet and a little numb, not overtly crying, but your voice catches when you mention his name. You did not think about the prescription until you saw the pickup text message today and came in almost on autopilot. If the pharmacist is warm and unhurried, you will share a little about your father, such as that he was stubborn about taking his pills on time. If the pharmacist is brisk, transactional, or asks logistical questions before acknowledging the loss, you become withdrawn and give only short answers. You are not sure what to do with the medication and may ask if it can be donated or if you should just throw it away.

checklist.item: Offers a sincere, brief condolence before moving into any logistical or procedural discussion
Speaks in a slower, softer pace appropriate to the emotional weight of the situation
Allows silence or pauses rather than rushing the family member through the conversation
Checks in on how the family member is doing rather than treating this purely as a pickup transaction
Provides clear, compassionate guidance on medication disposal options
Family member is treated with dignity and the interaction does not feel rushed or clinical

model_answer: A strong candidate begins with a brief, genuine expression of sympathy before saying anything about the prescription itself, and lets the family member set the pace of the conversation rather than rushing to logistics. The candidate speaks softly, uses the deceased patient's name if offered, and tolerates natural pauses without filling every silence. When the family member asks what to do with the medication, the candidate gives clear, kind guidance on safe disposal or take-back options without making the moment feel transactional. The candidate also gently checks whether the family member needs anything else, such as canceling other pending prescriptions for the deceased, while keeping the tone supportive rather than administrative throughout.

title: Anxious Patient Newly Diagnosed With Type 2 Diabetes
candidate_instructions: A patient was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes yesterday and is picking up metformin for the first time. You have 7 minutes to counsel the patient, address their visible anxiety, and support them in starting this new medication.
materials_provided: New prescription for metformin 500 mg, once daily with dinner, titrating as directed by the physician.
sp_role_script: You are Dennis Park, 47, who found out yesterday you have type 2 diabetes after a routine blood test. You feel scared and overwhelmed, worried this means you will need insulin shots soon or that you did this to yourself through bad habits. Your opening line is asking if this means your life is basically over now. Speak quickly and ramble a little, jumping between fears about complications, guilt about your diet, and confusion about what the pill actually does. If the pharmacist responds with calm, clear, reassuring information and acknowledges your fear first, you visibly relax and start asking more practical questions. If the pharmacist launches straight into technical dosing details without addressing your emotional state, your anxiety increases and you talk over them with more worried questions.

checklist.item: Acknowledges the patient's fear and normalizes the emotional reaction to a new diagnosis before diving into instructions
Uses plain, non-alarming language when explaining what metformin does and why it was prescribed
Breaks information into small, manageable pieces rather than delivering it all at once
Checks understanding using a teach-back style question before ending the conversation
Addresses at least one specific fear the patient raises, such as progression to insulin, with accurate reassurance
Patient leaves calmer and with a clear, realistic sense of the next steps

model_answer: A strong candidate starts by naming the patient's fear directly, saying something like it is completely normal to feel overwhelmed right after a new diagnosis, before offering any clinical information. The candidate then explains in simple terms that metformin helps the body use insulin more effectively and is a common, well-studied first step, deliberately avoiding the assumption that diagnosis equals personal failure. Information is delivered in small chunks, checking the patient's reaction after each point rather than reciting a full counseling script. The candidate directly addresses the fear about insulin injections by explaining that many people manage type 2 diabetes for years with oral medication and lifestyle changes alone. Before ending, the candidate asks the patient to describe in their own words when and how they will take the medication to confirm understanding.

title: Skeptical Patient Distrustful of Medications
candidate_instructions: A patient with newly diagnosed hypertension has not been filling their prescribed medication and expresses distrust of pharmacists and pharmaceutical companies. You have 7 minutes to explore the patient's concerns using a motivational interviewing approach and support them toward an informed decision, without pressuring them.
materials_provided: Profile note showing the antihypertensive prescription was sent three weeks ago and has never been picked up.
sp_role_script: You are Gary Whitfield, 55, and you believe most prescription medications are pushed by companies more interested in profit than health. You have read stories online about side effects being hidden from patients. Your opening line is that you have decided you do not want to fill this blood pressure pill and you are managing fine with diet changes. You are polite but firm, and you bristle if you feel lectured or dismissed. If the pharmacist asks open questions and listens without arguing, you gradually reveal that you are actually a little worried about your numbers and open to hearing more. If the pharmacist argues, cites statistics aggressively, or implies you are wrong to be skeptical, you become more defensive and repeat that you have made up your mind.

checklist.item: Uses open-ended questions to explore the patient's specific concerns rather than immediately countering them
Reflects back the patient's stated concerns to demonstrate understanding before offering information
Avoids arguing, lecturing, or using scare tactics about the risks of not treating hypertension
Elicits the patient's own reasons for possibly considering treatment rather than only providing the pharmacist's reasons
Offers balanced, factual information about the medication only after rapport and permission are established
Patient's autonomy is respected and the conversation ends without pressure or confrontation

model_answer: A strong candidate uses a motivational interviewing approach, opening with an open question about what specifically concerns the patient about the medication rather than immediately defending it. The candidate reflects the patient's stated worries back, such as noting concern that side effects are not always fully disclosed, which builds trust and reduces defensiveness. Rather than arguing with statistics, the candidate asks permission before sharing information, for example asking if it would be okay to share what is known about this particular medication's safety profile. The candidate elicits the patient's own motivations by asking what would need to be true for the patient to feel comfortable trying the medication, and highlights the patient's autonomy throughout. The conversation ends with a low-pressure next step, such as offering to check blood pressure readings again in a few weeks rather than insisting on immediate treatment.

title: Patient With a Health Literacy and Language Barrier
candidate_instructions: A patient with limited English proficiency and low health literacy has come to pick up a new prescription for a corticosteroid inhaler. You have 7 minutes to counsel the patient on proper use in a way they can understand, using plain language and confirming comprehension.
materials_provided: New prescription for a corticosteroid inhaler, twice daily, with instructions to rinse mouth after use.
sp_role_script: You are Linh Tran, 63, a recent immigrant who speaks English as a second language and has limited familiarity with medical terms. You understand simple, short sentences better than complex medical explanations, and you sometimes nod politely even when you have not fully understood, which is a cue the candidate should notice and check for genuine understanding rather than accept the nod at face value. Your opening line is a quiet, polite question asking how many times you take this. If the pharmacist uses complicated words like corticosteroid, oropharyngeal candidiasis, or rapid speech, look confused and just nod. If the pharmacist slows down, uses simple words, and demonstrates the inhaler technique with gestures or a demo device, you visibly relax, ask a clarifying question in simple words, and correctly repeat back the instructions when asked.

checklist.item: Uses short, simple sentences and avoids medical jargon throughout the explanation
Slows speech pace and uses visual or physical demonstration to support verbal instructions
Checks true understanding with a teach-back method rather than accepting a nod as confirmation
Remains patient and respectful, avoiding any tone that could feel condescending
Confirms the patient can correctly describe when and how to use the inhaler including the mouth rinse step
Patient demonstrates genuine understanding by the end of the encounter, not just polite agreement

model_answer: A strong candidate immediately simplifies their language, using short sentences and everyday words instead of clinical terms like corticosteroid, and paces their speech more slowly without becoming condescending. The candidate demonstrates the inhaler technique physically, using a demo device or gestures alongside simple verbal steps, since this supports understanding beyond words alone. Recognizing that a nod does not confirm true comprehension, the candidate uses a genuine teach-back approach, asking the patient to show or describe how they will use the inhaler at home. When the patient's demonstration reveals a gap, such as forgetting the mouth rinse step, the candidate calmly repeats and reinforces that specific point rather than moving on. Throughout, the candidate maintains a warm, respectful tone that treats the patient as capable rather than talking down to them.

title: Spouse Requesting Confidential Prescription Information
candidate_instructions: A person at the counter asks about their spouse's prescription history and current medications. You have 7 minutes to handle this request in a way that respects patient confidentiality while remaining respectful and helpful to the person in front of you.
materials_provided: None beyond the instructions above.
sp_role_script: You are Carol Jennings, 52, and you are asking what medications your husband Robert has been picking up lately, saying you are worried he might be hiding a health problem from you. Your opening line is asking the pharmacist to just tell you what he has been filling since you are his wife and it should not be a big secret. You are not angry at first, just concerned and a little insistent. If the pharmacist explains clearly and kindly that they cannot share another person's prescription details without consent, but offers an alternative such as encouraging a conversation with your husband or offering to speak with him together, you accept this with some disappointment but understanding. If the pharmacist is cold, cites policy without warmth, or does not offer any alternative path, you become frustrated and accuse the pharmacy of not caring about family concerns.

checklist.item: Clearly and kindly explains that prescription information is confidential and cannot be shared without the patient's consent
Acknowledges the spouse's underlying concern and worry rather than dismissing it
Avoids confirming or denying any specific details about the other patient's medications, even indirectly
Offers a constructive alternative, such as encouraging a joint conversation or suggesting the spouse speak with the patient directly
Maintains the patient's confidentiality throughout the entire interaction without any accidental disclosure
The person asking leaves with their concern acknowledged even though no confidential information was shared

model_answer: A strong candidate acknowledges the spouse's genuine worry before explaining the confidentiality limitation, saying something like understanding that it is difficult to feel shut out when you are concerned about a loved one's health. The candidate then clearly but kindly explains that prescription information is private to each patient and cannot be shared without their consent, avoiding any policy jargon that would feel cold. Importantly, the candidate does not confirm or deny any specific details about the husband's medications, even in an indirect way that could reveal information. Rather than ending the conversation there, the candidate offers a constructive path forward, such as suggesting the spouse have an open conversation at home or offering to meet with both of them together if the husband consents. This approach protects confidentiality while still leaving the spouse feeling supported rather than stonewalled.

title: Rushed and Dismissive Patient at Pickup
setting: Community pharmacy front counter, busy period
candidate_instructions: A patient picking up a new prescription for an anticoagulant is clearly in a hurry and dismissive of counseling. You have 7 minutes to re-engage the patient enough to convey the essential safety information before they leave.
materials_provided: New prescription for an oral anticoagulant with important bleeding precaution counseling points.
sp_role_script: You are Steve Malhotra, 61, picking up a new blood thinner prescription on your lunch break. You are checking your phone and your opening line, without looking up, is asking the pharmacist to just hurry because you are double parked and already know how to take pills. You give short, clipped answers and try to end the conversation quickly. If the pharmacist tries to deliver a long, unprompted counseling script, you interrupt and say you really have to go. If the pharmacist instead briefly and directly flags that this is a new type of medication with a specific bleeding risk that is worth thirty seconds of your time, you pause, put your phone down, and listen to the key points, then ask one genuine question about whether you can still take ibuprofen.

checklist.item: Respects the patient's time constraint while still conveying the encounter has genuine importance
Uses a brief, attention-getting opening statement rather than launching into a full counseling script
Prioritizes the single most critical safety point given the limited time available
Reads the patient's cues and adjusts pace and depth of information accordingly
Successfully conveys the key bleeding risk and interaction warning before the patient leaves
Patient's autonomy and time pressure are respected while essential safety information is not skipped

model_answer: A strong candidate recognizes early that a full standard counseling script will not land with this patient and instead opens with a brief, direct hook, such as saying this is a new type of medication with one important safety point worth thirty seconds. This respects the patient's time pressure while signaling genuine importance rather than routine paperwork. The candidate prioritizes the single highest-risk point, the increased bleeding risk and the need to avoid certain over-the-counter pain relievers like ibuprofen, rather than trying to cover every detail. By reading the patient's body language, such as noticing when the phone goes down as a cue of engagement, the candidate adjusts pace and stops once the key point lands rather than continuing to talk. This approach successfully transmits the critical safety information without feeling like it disrespects the patient's stated time constraint.

title: Overwhelmed Caregiver Managing a Complex Regimen
candidate_instructions: A caregiver managing her elderly mother's nine medications appears overwhelmed and admits doses have been missed or doubled up. You have 7 minutes to explore the caregiver's challenges and offer practical support.
materials_provided: Medication list showing nine regularly scheduled medications for the caregiver's mother, with varying dosing times.
sp_role_script: You are Angela Reyes, 44, caring for your 82 year old mother who has dementia and takes nine different medications at different times of day. You are exhausted and a little tearful, admitting that last week you gave a double dose of one medication by accident because you lost track. Your opening line is that you honestly do not know how you are supposed to keep all of this straight anymore. You speak quickly, listing frustrations about work, your mother's confusion, and guilt about the mistake. If the pharmacist listens patiently and offers practical tools like a blister pack or simplified schedule, you feel relieved and grateful. If the pharmacist responds with clinical judgment about the error or rushes past your emotional state, you become more anxious and self-critical, repeating that you are failing your mother.

checklist.item: Responds to the caregiver's admission of a dosing error with empathy rather than judgment
Allows the caregiver space to express frustration and exhaustion before moving to solutions
Uses a collaborative tone when proposing tools such as blister packaging or simplified schedules
Validates the difficulty of managing a complex regimen rather than implying it should be easy
Identifies at least one practical, realistic strategy to reduce the risk of future dosing errors
Caregiver leaves with reduced guilt and a concrete plan rather than feeling blamed

model_answer: A strong candidate responds to the disclosed dosing error with a non-judgmental, empathetic statement, acknowledging that managing nine medications on different schedules for someone else is genuinely difficult and that mistakes are understandable under that kind of strain. The candidate allows the caregiver to vent frustration and exhaustion without interrupting to problem solve too quickly, since being heard is part of what she needs first. Once the emotional content has been acknowledged, the candidate collaboratively introduces practical tools such as a blister pack, a simplified once or twice daily regimen where clinically possible, or a written schedule, framing these as ways to reduce burden rather than corrections to a failure. The candidate explicitly validates that the caregiver's difficulty does not reflect poor caregiving, and ends with one concrete, realistic next step the caregiver can act on immediately.

title: Patient Whose Personal Beliefs Conflict With Recommended Therapy
candidate_instructions: A patient has been prescribed a blood product derived medication and expresses a personal belief that conflicts with taking it. You have 7 minutes to discuss the situation respectfully, explore options, and support an informed decision without imposing your own views.
materials_provided: New prescription note indicating the medication in question contains a blood derived component, with a suitable alternative available on request from the prescriber.
sp_role_script: You are Thomas Bekele, 39, and you have just learned the medication your doctor prescribed contains a blood derived component, which conflicts with a personal and religious belief you hold strongly. Your opening line is explaining, calmly but firmly, that you cannot take this medication because of your beliefs and asking what your options are. You are not looking for the pharmacist to argue with your belief, only to help you find a path forward. If the pharmacist responds respectfully, without minimizing or arguing with your belief, and explores alternatives or offers to contact the prescriber, you are appreciative and cooperative. If the pharmacist questions or challenges your belief, or implies you are being unreasonable, you become guarded and repeat that this is not up for debate.

checklist.item: Responds to the patient's belief with respect and without judgment or debate
Avoids minimizing or questioning the validity of the patient's personal or religious belief
Uses collaborative language when exploring alternative therapy options
Clarifies the patient's goals and concerns before proposing a specific solution
Identifies a concrete next step, such as contacting the prescriber about an alternative medication
Patient's autonomy and beliefs are respected while safe, effective care is still pursued

model_answer: A strong candidate responds to the patient's disclosure with immediate respect, thanking the patient for sharing something personal and making clear the belief will be honored rather than debated. The candidate avoids any language that questions or minimizes the belief, focusing instead on practical next steps. The candidate asks clarifying questions about what the patient needs, then explains that an alternative medication without the blood derived component may be available and offers to contact the prescriber to arrange it. Throughout, the candidate keeps the tone collaborative and non-judgmental, framing the conversation as a partnership to find a therapy that works both clinically and personally for the patient. The encounter ends with a clear, concrete plan, such as the pharmacist calling the prescriber that day.

title: Teenage Patient Seeking Confidential Contraception Counseling
candidate_instructions: A 17 year old patient approaches you privately to ask about emergency contraception and wants to know if her parents will find out. You have 7 minutes to counsel her in an age-appropriate, non-judgmental, and confidential manner consistent with her right to privacy for this type of request.
materials_provided: None beyond the instructions above.
sp_role_script: You are Ava Simmons, 17, quietly asking if you can get the morning after pill without your parents finding out. You are nervous and speak in a hushed voice, glancing toward the door. Your opening line is asking if this is something you can just buy without anyone telling your parents. If the pharmacist is calm, non-judgmental, and explains your privacy rights and how the medication works clearly, you relax and ask a couple of practical questions like timing and what to do if you vomit after taking it. If the pharmacist seems shocked, judgmental, or implies you need parental involvement, you become embarrassed, shut down, and say you will just figure it out online instead.

checklist.item: Responds in a calm, non-judgmental tone that puts the teenage patient at ease
Clearly explains the patient's right to confidential access for this request in age-appropriate language
Avoids any tone or facial cue suggesting shock, disapproval, or moralizing
Uses simple, clear language appropriate for a teenage patient when explaining how and when to take the medication
Provides accurate information on timing, effectiveness, and what to do if a dose is vomited
Patient leaves feeling safe, respected, and adequately informed rather than embarrassed or judged

model_answer: A strong candidate immediately adopts a calm, matter of fact tone with no visible surprise or judgment, which is essential to keeping a nervous teenage patient engaged rather than causing her to shut down. The candidate clearly and simply explains that this request can be handled confidentially and that her privacy is respected, directly answering her underlying fear before moving into clinical details. Information about timing, how the medication works, and what to do if vomiting occurs shortly after taking it is delivered in short, plain language appropriate for a teenage patient, checking her understanding along the way. The candidate avoids any moralizing language or unnecessary questions about the circumstances, keeping the focus strictly on accurate information and her safety. The encounter closes with an invitation for her to return with any further questions, reinforcing that she is welcome and not judged.

title: Disclosing a Prior Dispensing Error to a Patient
candidate_instructions: During a routine review you discover that a patient was given the wrong strength of a medication on their last fill, one week ago. The patient has come in for their next refill. You have 7 minutes to disclose the error, explain what happened, and address the patient's reaction appropriately.
